sections.t: This is my field guide to the craft of product management: the competencies that define the job at every level, how to actually assess and grow them, and how AI is rewriting the work in real time. I have spent more than ten years building B2C mobile products in sports, media, and betting, and for most of that time I was also hiring, leveling, and coaching PMs. The frameworks here are not borrowed slides. They are the system I have built and used, and I will tell you what I believe and why. [!tip] 👋 New here? Read What Is Product Management for my definition of the job, then The PM Competency Model for the spine of this whole guide, then How to Use This Knowledge Base to navigate.

Domain Start at What you'll find 🧱 Foundations Foundations of the Craft What the job is, The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn\, teams, and Product Sense and Judgment\ 🛠️ Product Execution Product Execution Feature Specification, Product Delivery, Product Quality, and The 10 50 90 Execution Framework\ 🔍 Customer Insight Customer Insight Fluency with Data\, Voice of the Customer\, Continuous Discovery\, and User Experience Design\ 🎯 Product Strategy Product Strategy Crafting a Product Vision\, Building and Managing a Roadmap\, Prioritization Frameworks\, and OKRs and Goal Setting\ 🤝 Influencing People Influencing People Stakeholder Management, Managing Up, Communication, and Leading Without Authority\ 🪜 The Career Ladder The Product Career Ladder Associate Product Manager\ up to The Chief Product Officer\, and The IC vs Manager Decision\ 📊 Assessment & Coaching Assessment and Coaching The Competency Self Assessment\, Score Gated Leveling\, and Building a Growth Plan\ 🧰 Frameworks Toolkit The Frameworks Toolkit The Kano Model\, The LNO Framework\, theScore Product Principles\, and more 🤖 AI & the Modern PM AI and the Modern PM Writing Evals for AI Products\, The AI Product Manager\, and Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates\ 📚 Reference Reference The Master Competency Matrix\, Glossary of Product Management Terms\, and The Product Management Canon\

A product manager is accountable for whether a product succeeds . Not for writing tickets, owning a backlog, or shipping features on schedule. The job lives where what users need, what the business needs, and what is technically possible all meet, and the real work is judgment under uncertainty : deciding which problems are worth solving, then rallying a team to solve them in a way customers love and the business can sustain. I organize that ability into four competency areas, Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, and Influencing People, which together make up The PM Competency Model this guide is built on.

🆕 Deciding if PM is for you → What Is Product Management · Types of Product Managers 📈 Leveling up → The Product Career Ladder · The Competency Self Assessment 🤖 Worried about AI → How AI Is Reshaping Product Management · Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates 🧰 Want a specific framework → The Frameworks Toolkit · Prioritization Frameworks

Here is the definition I will defend: a product manager is accountable for whether a product succeeds. Not for writing tickets, not for "owning the backlog," not for shipping a feature list on time. Those are activities. Success is an outcome, and the PM owns the outcome. The job sits at the intersection of three forces, what users need, what the business needs, and what is technically possible . The real work is judgment under uncertainty: choosing which problems are worth solving, then getting a team to solve them in a way customers love and the business can sustain. Everything in The PM Competency Model is downstream of that one sentence.

[!warning] The "mini CEO" framing You will hear that a PM is the "CEO of the product," a line Ben Horowitz popularized. I think it is half right and a little dangerous, and it is the framing I push back on most with new PMs. The half that is right: you carry the accountability. The half that gets people in trouble: you have almost none of the authority. You cannot hire, fire, or order anyone to do anything. That gap is the whole game, and you close it by leading through influence rather than control. See Leading Without Authority. Two more things the job is not. It is not the Product Manager vs Adjacent Roles role. Running a backlog and writing stories is maybe 10% of the work, and it is the most automatable 10% at that (more in AI in Prototyping and Delivery). It is also not project management. A PM who only ships a predefined plan on a delivery track is, in my experience, a project manager with a fancier title. The thing that separates the two is ownership of the plan being right , not just the plan getting executed .

The clearest mental model is the overlap of disciplines, which I lay out in The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn. The PM does not do the design or the engineering. What the PM owns is the trade offs between them (Understanding Trade offs), and accountability for two of The Four Big Product Risks: is it valuable , and is it viable for the business. The other thing that separates good from great is an obsession with outcomes over outputs (Outcomes Over Outputs). Feature factories measure themselves by what they ship. Real product teams measure themselves by what changes for the user and the business. Which kind of team you are on tends to be the single best predictor of whether the product will work, and I unpack that in Product Teams vs Feature Teams.

sections.h: Why it is hard to pin down
sections.t: PM is defined inconsistently from company to company, which is exactly why a clear competency model and career ladder matter so much. The role flexes with company stage (Product Management Across Company Stages) and with specialization (Types of Product Managers). An early stage generalist and a platform PM at a 5,000 person company share a title and not much else. The constant underneath all of it is Product Sense and Judgment: being right, more often than chance, about what to build.

This is the spine of the whole Atlas. Over years of leveling and coaching PMs, I converged on a model with four competency areas , each holding three competencies , for twelve in total. It is adapted from the Reforge and Ravi Mehta "Peak Product Manager" model, which is the closest thing our field has to a complete definition of the job. What I have added is the part that makes it usable day to day: a verbatim set of behaviors under each competency, a career ladder mapped to it, and a leveling system that actually gates promotions (Score Gated Leveling).

sections.h: The four areas and twelve competencies
sections.t: Area What it means The three competencies Product Execution Define, build, and launch exceptional products Feature Specification · Product Delivery · Product Quality Customer Insight Understand and fulfill customer needs Fluency with Data · Voice of the Customer · User Experience Design Product Strategy Deliver business impact via product innovation Business Outcome Ownership · Strategic Impact · Product Vision and Roadmapping Influencing People Rally people around the team's work Stakeholder Management · Team Leadership · Managing Up There is a rough arc here. Execution is where most careers start, and strategy and influence are where they mature. Treat that as a center of gravity, not a rule. Which brings me to the most important idea in the model.

sections.h: You are spiky, not well rounded
sections.t: [!tip] Know your shape Almost nobody is strong across all twelve, and you do not need to be. The best PMs I have worked with are spiky . They are exceptional at three or four competencies and competent at the rest, and they win by knowing their shape and building teams that fill the gaps. Chasing a flat, uniformly "high" profile is a good way to end up mediocre at everything. More in The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape.

sections.h: Levels: the same competencies, a higher bar
sections.t: The twelve competencies do not change as you climb. The altitude does. I map progress on a six stage mastery spine, running from Gaining Experience to Recognized Thought Leader , detailed in The Six Stages of Mastery. The same skill, say Feature Specification, means "writes a clear spec for one feature with help" at the Associate Product Manager level and "improves the spec process for every team" at the VP level. The full progression lives in The Product Career Ladder, and the at a glance grid in The Master Competency Matrix.

sections.h: Where this model is thin (and how I patch it)
sections.t: I would rather be honest than dogmatic about this. A clean 4x12 grid under weights a few things, so I treat these as first class even though they do not sit neatly in one box: discovery as its own discipline (Continuous Discovery, The Four Big Product Risks), product sense (Product Sense and Judgment), technical and GTM fluency, and, loudest of all right now, the way AI is rewriting which competencies even matter (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates).

title: How to Use This Knowledge Base

sections.t: The Atlas is a connected web, not a linear book, but it still rewards a starting point. Pick the path that matches where you are right now.

sections.h: Read it by where you are
sections.t: [!example] Pick your path "Is PM even for me?" → What Is Product Management → Types of Product Managers → Foundations of the Craft. "I'm an APM/PM leveling up." → The PM Competency Model → run The Competency Self Assessment → go deep on your two weakest competencies → Building a Growth Plan. "I manage PMs." → Assessment and Coaching → Score Gated Leveling → The Manager as Coach → Calibration and Promotion. "What does AI do to this job?" → How AI Is Reshaping Product Management → Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates → The AI Product Manager. "I need a specific framework, now." → The Frameworks Toolkit or Prioritization Frameworks.

Every note runs 350 to 700 words, stays cross linked, and ends with a Continue Reading list so you can follow a thread wherever it leads. The ten domains after this one each open with a hub note (like Product Execution or The Product Career Ladder) that maps everything inside it. If you would rather browse the whole structure at once, the Home page and The Master Competency Matrix are your overviews. The four pillar domains are the heart of the Atlas: Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, and Influencing People. Each of their twelve competency notes follows the same shape. 1. What it is , meaning my definition of the competency. 2. The behaviors , or what it actually looks like in practice. 3. How it shows up by level , from Associate Product Manager up to VP. 4. How to grow it , which is the concrete, opinionated coaching. 5. How AI is changing it , because in 2026 that section is not optional.

This guide is opinionated on purpose . I state what I believe and why, because a reference that refuses to take a position is useless the moment you actually have to decide something. Where I am giving you my take rather than settled fact, you will know. Where popular belief is a Myths and Misconceptions, I say so. Disagree freely. Knowing why you disagree is itself a sign your Product Sense and Judgment is developing, and that is the whole point.

This is a map, not the territory. No framework survives contact with a real org chart, a real deadline, and a real executive who has already made up their mind. Use the competencies as a shared language for growth rather than a rubric to hide behind, and treat The Frameworks Toolkit as tools to reach for, never boxes to fill in. If you would rather just ask, the "Ask the Guide" question box (press ) answers in plain language straight from these notes.

Before you can grow a competency, you have to know what the job is . This domain is the bedrock the twelve competencies sit on. It holds the shared definitions, distinctions, and mental models I assume you already carry by the time we talk about Feature Specification or Product Vision and Roadmapping. Skip these and the competency notes will read like a checklist. Read these and they read like a craft. I built The PM Competency Model as the skeleton of this Atlas, and a skeleton needs ground to stand on. These thirteen notes are that ground: what a PM actually does, how the role differs from the ones it gets confused with, the trade offs at its core, the kinds of teams and orgs PMs work inside, and the judgment that separates a good PM from a great one.

Product management is the discipline of being accountable for the outcome of a product without owning the people or the code that produce it. You sit in the overlap of product, design, and engineering (The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn), and your core act is making and defending Understanding Trade offs across the The Four Big Product Risks. You do all of this through influence rather than authority, which is why the whole back half of this Atlas is about Influencing People. Everything else is detail.

Note What it gives you The Product Manager Role What a PM actually does day to day and quarter to quarter, accountable for outcomes rather than output. Product Manager vs Adjacent Roles PM vs Product Owner vs Product Marketing vs Project Manager. The distinctions people get wrong. The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn The classic intersection, and why the PM owns the overlap and the trade offs. Understanding Trade offs The PM's core act: value, usability, feasibility, and viability held in tension. Product Teams vs Feature Teams Cagan's distinction: empowered outcome teams vs output driven feature factories. The Product Organization Matrix Outcome vs Feature teams crossed with Owner vs Facilitator. Where your specific job lives. Types of Product Managers Growth, platform, B2B vs B2C, 0 to 1, AI PM. The role is not one job. The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape Spiky, not well rounded. Build teams that cover your gaps. Product Management Across Company Stages How the job changes from startup to scaleup to enterprise. Character, Competency, and Craft The hard to teach to easy to teach spectrum, and why character comes first. Product Sense and Judgment Taste, trainable judgment, being right more often than chance. The Four Big Product Risks Value, usability, feasibility, viability, and who owns each.

If you're new to the discipline, read top to bottom. The order is deliberate. If you're experienced, three notes earn your time even if you skip the rest: The Four Big Product Risks (the cleanest model of what you're actually managing), The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape (the honest version of "what am I good at"), and Character, Competency, and Craft (why your best hire might be the one who interviews worst on frameworks). [!warning] The myth this domain kills That product management is a single, well defined job you can master with a certification. It isn't. It's a shape of accountability that bends to the company, the stage, the team type, and the product. The foundations hold steady. Everything built on them moves.

Ask ten people what a PM does and you'll get ten job descriptions. Here's mine, sharpened over a decade in B2C mobile: a product manager is accountable for the outcome of a product, and produces almost none of it directly. You don't design the screens, write the code, run the ad campaign, or close the sale. You are responsible for whether the thing works, for the business and for the user, and you get there through other people. Hold that contradiction in your head and most of the job starts to make sense.

You'll hear the PM described as "CEO of the product," a line Ben Horowitz popularized, and I'd retire it. A CEO has authority. They hire, they fire, they allocate. A PM has none of that. What we share with a CEO is accountability without a hiding place : when the product fails, "engineering was late" is not an answer you get to give. So the comparison is half right and a little dangerous, and it's the framing I push back on most with new PMs, because they hear "CEO" and start issuing orders instead of building the influence the job actually runs on (Leading Without Authority). I'd rather you think of yourself as the person who makes sure the right product gets built and that everyone knows why.

The day to day and the quarter to quarter are different jobs, and conflating them is why PMs feel busy and unproductive at the same time. [!note] Day to day (the execution loop) Unblock the team. Clarify the spec when an edge case surfaces. Make the small decision that's holding up a build. Triage a bug against the roadmap. Sit in standup and listen for the thing nobody's saying. This is the daily work of delivery, and most of it is removing friction so engineers and designers can do their best work. If your calendar is wall to wall here, you're a high functioning The Product Organization Matrix. Useful, but not yet steering. [!note] Quarter to quarter (the outcome loop) Decide what's worth building and why. Run Continuous Discovery so you're solving real problems. Set and defend the roadmap. Connect the work to a measurable business outcome. Say no, repeatedly, to good ideas that aren't the best idea. This is where the leverage is, and it's the first thing that gets crowded out by the day to day. The trap is that the execution loop is urgent and the outcome loop is merely important. Protect the outcome loop or you'll spend your career as a very efficient order taker.

The single most important sentence in this Atlas: you are measured by outcomes, not output. Shipping ten features is output. Moving retention three points is an outcome. A team can ship constantly and change nothing, which is what a Product Teams vs Feature Teams does. The whole point of the role is to be on the hook for the result , which is why I made Outcomes Over Outputs its own note and why it threads through every competency.

sections.h: The four areas, in one breath
sections.t: What you're accountable for maps cleanly to The PM Competency Model. You execute (define, build, launch, the whole of Product Execution), you understand customers (Customer Insight), you set strategy (Product Strategy), and you influence people (Influencing People) to make all three happen. Four jobs in one. Nobody is equally good at all four, which is the point of The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape. [!tip] The honest test of whether you're doing the role At the end of a quarter, can you point to an outcome you owned, name the trade offs you made to get there, and explain why to anyone in the company? If yes, you're a PM. If you can only point to a list of things that shipped, you're running someone else's roadmap.

Four roles share three letters or a word with "product manager," and they get conflated with it constantly: Product Owner, Product Marketing Manager, Project Manager, and the engineering driven "Technical Product Manager." Getting these wrong is not pedantry. It is how PMs end up doing the wrong job well, grooming a backlog or writing release notes or chasing a Gantt chart, while the actual product question goes unasked. Here is how I draw the lines, and why the distinctions are worth keeping straight.

This is the one that matters most, so I lean on Marty Cagan, who has been clear about it for years: Product Owner is a role in a Scrum process, not a job. His framing is that the PO responsibilities are roughly 10% of a real product manager's job , and that is the cleanest correction I know. I want to be fair about the other view, though. In the Scrum and SAFe world, Product Owner is treated as a substantial standalone role with real responsibility, and plenty of people hold that title and do serious work. My point is not that the work is trivial. It is that the PO slice is delivery hygiene: maintaining the backlog, clarifying stories for the sprint, accepting work. All of it is necessary. But if that is the whole of what you do, you are working someone else's decisions instead of making your own. The other 90% is the product job: discovery, strategy, the The Four Big Product Risks, the outcomes. Cagan has a hard rule that you never split discovery from delivery, and it exists for a reason. When you make "PO" a separate person from "PM," you sever the thinking from the doing, and you get a Product Teams vs Feature Teams with extra ceremony bolted on. [!warning] The PO trap In my experience, "Product Owner" as a permanent title is one of the surest signs of a company that has confused agile process with product strategy. As Cagan notes, it is also the part of the role most exposed to AI, because backlog grooming and ticket writing are exactly what automation does well. I would be careful about building a whole career there.

A project manager is accountable for delivering a defined scope on time and on budget. A product manager is accountable for deciding what the scope should be and whether it is worth doing at all. The project manager optimizes the plan. The PM owns whether there should be a plan. They are complements, not substitutes, and on large programs you want both. Conflate them and either nobody owns the timeline or nobody owns the why . If your role is to make sure the thing ships on the date, you are doing valuable project management. Just do not mistake it for product.

Product Marketing (PMM) owns positioning, messaging, go to market, and the narrative that reaches the market. The PM owns what gets built and why. The seam between them is Shipping and Launch, and it is where great products quietly die: built well, positioned poorly. I treat PMM as my closest cross functional partner on the Competitive Landscape Analysis and commercialization side. The line I draw is simple. PM is accountable for the product outcome . PMM is accountable for the market's understanding of it.

Role Accountable for Time horizon The trap Product Manager The outcome: the right product, built, that works for users and the business Quarter to years Doing the PO 10% and calling it the job Product Owner Backlog and sprint delivery (a Scrum role) Sprint Treating it as a standalone career Project Manager Defined scope, on time and budget Project Owning the when but not the why Product Marketing Positioning, messaging, GTM Launch and campaign Selling a product the PM mis scoped Technical PM Product for technical users and platform Quarter to years Becoming an eng proxy instead of a product owner (see Types of Product Managers) [!tip] The test If you can be fully evaluated on whether things shipped on time and to spec, you are holding an adjacent role. The PM is the only one on this list who can ship everything on time and still have failed, because the product did not move the Outcomes Over Outputs.

title: The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn

sections.t: The most useful one picture answer to "what is a PM" is three overlapping circles, Product, Design, and Engineering , with the PM sitting in the middle. It is a cliche diagram for a reason. It is right. But almost everyone reads it wrong. The middle is not where you do a third of each discipline's job. The middle is where the trade offs live, and the PM's job is to own the overlap, not to be a mediocre designer and a junior engineer at the same time.

[!note] The three circles Engineering owns "can we build it, and how": feasibility, architecture, the cost of the work. Design owns "can people use it, and should it feel this way": usability, interaction, craft. Product (you) owns "should we build it, and is it worth it": value to the user and viability for the business. Each discipline has a circle of genuine authority. You do not overrule your tech lead on architecture or your designer on interaction patterns, any more than they overrule you on what is worth building. Respecting the circles is what earns you the right to stand in the middle.

The overlap is not empty space. It is the hardest space. It is where "the cleanest design" meets "a six week build" meets "a feature only 4% of users want." Someone has to hold all three truths at once and decide. That someone is the PM. Teresa Torres calls the PM, design, and engineering unit the product trio and argues the three should discover together. I agree, with one caveat. Discovering together does not dissolve accountability. When the trio cannot agree, the PM owns the call on what is worth building, the designer owns how it works, and the tech lead owns how it is built. Shared discovery, clear ownership. The Product and Design overlap specifically is richer than people think. In my experience it is where service design, information architecture, research synthesis, stakeholder facilitation, concept modeling, and the writing of user stories actually happen. That work belongs to neither discipline cleanly, and a good PM and a good designer do it together . See User Experience Design and Design Sense and Critique for where my circle and design's circle genuinely blend.

This is the whole point, so I will say it plainly. The value you add in the middle is Understanding Trade offs nobody else is positioned to make. Engineering optimizes for feasibility and maintainability. Design optimizes for usability and craft. Both are right to. Left alone, each would build a different product. The PM is the only seat with the full context (user value, business viability, technical cost, design quality), and therefore the only seat that can make the call across all four. That maps directly onto The Four Big Product Risks: value and viability are yours , usability is design's, feasibility is engineering's. [!warning] The two ways PMs misread the Venn 1. The wannabe specialist. Tries to be a part time designer and part time engineer, redlines mockups, writes pseudo code. You will be worse than the specialists and you will insult them. Stay in your circle and own the overlap. 2. The empty middle. Sits in the center and just relays messages between the other two circles, a router rather than a decision maker. That is a The Product Organization Matrix, not an owner. The center is where you decide , not where you forward .

If I had to compress the PM job into two words, it would be managing trade offs . Not generating ideas, because ideas are cheap and everyone has them. Not shipping, because engineers ship. The irreducible PM act is choosing, under constraint, which good thing to sacrifice for which better thing, and then being able to defend the choice. A PM who cannot articulate the trade off they made has not made a decision. They have had one happen to them.

sections.h: Why trade offs are the core act
sections.t: Every interesting product question is a trade off, because resources are finite and the dimensions of "good" conflict. The fastest to build solution is rarely the most usable. The most usable is rarely the most profitable. The most profitable this quarter often mortgages next year. There is almost never a choice that wins on every axis, and when there appears to be one, you usually have not found the real constraint yet. The PM sits in the seat with the full context (The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn), which makes them the only person positioned to weigh the axes against each other.

sections.h: The four axes you are trading across
sections.t: The cleanest decomposition comes from Marty Cagan's The Four Big Product Risks. Every real product decision is a trade off among these four: Axis The question Who pushes hardest for it Value Will customers want it enough to choose it? PM Usability Can they figure out how to use it? Design Feasibility Can we build it, and at what cost? Engineering Viability Does it work for our business, across margin, legal, brand, and GTM? PM The PM owns value and viability outright and arbitrates when usability and feasibility pull against them. A designer fighting for one more iteration is optimizing usability. A tech lead pushing for a simpler architecture is optimizing feasibility. Both are doing their jobs. Your job is to decide when "good enough" on one axis buys you something that matters more on another.

sections.h: How to actually make the call
sections.t: Trade offs feel like gut calls, and partly they are. That is Product Sense and Judgment. But the gut should be informed by a method. [!tip] My trade off checklist 1. Name the axes in tension. Out loud. "We are trading two weeks of build time for a 15% conversion lift we think exists." Naming it kills half the bad arguments. 2. Quantify what you can, label what you can't. Effort is usually estimable. Value is usually a hypothesis. Be honest about which numbers are real (Fluency with Data) and which are guesses. 3. Decide on the highest leverage axis, not the loudest voice. The person who argues hardest is not the axis that matters most. 4. Make it reversible if you can. A two way door decision deserves a fast, cheap call. A one way door deserves more rigor. Most PMs over deliberate reversible calls and under deliberate irreversible ones. This is the connective tissue to Sound Product Decision Making and the whole of Prioritization Frameworks. RICE, the The Kano Model, and the The Eisenhower Matrix are all just structured ways to make trade offs legible.

sections.h: Communicate the trade off, not just the decision
sections.t: The decision is the cheap part. The expensive, high leverage part is explaining the "why" so the team commits instead of complies. When an engineer understands which axis you optimized and what you gave up, they stop second guessing and start helping. When they only hear the verdict, you have created a quiet skeptic. This is why "explain the why behind trade offs" sits inside Product Delivery as a named behavior. It is not soft skills garnish. It is how the decision actually sticks. [!warning] The myth of the win win Be wary of anyone who frames a hard product call as a pure win win. Real trade offs have a cost, and pretending otherwise just hides the cost from the people who will pay it later. "What are we giving up?" is the most useful question in the room, and usually the one nobody wants to ask.

This is, to me, the single most clarifying distinction in modern product thinking, and it is Marty Cagan's. Two teams can look identical from the outside, with the same headcount, the same standups, the same Jira board, and still be doing fundamentally different work. One is handed a roadmap of features and asked to build them on schedule. The other is handed a problem and a measurable outcome and trusted to figure out the solution. Cagan calls the first a feature team and the second a product team (or empowered team), and once you see the difference you cannot unsee it.

[!quote] Cagan's framing Feature teams are measured on output: did they ship the roadmap. Empowered product teams are measured on outcomes: did they solve the problem and move the metric. The first is a "feature factory" (John Cutler's term). The second is what good looks like. Feature Team Product Team (Empowered) Given A roadmap of features A problem and an outcome to move Measured on Did we ship it (output) Did we solve it (Outcomes Over Outputs\) Discovery Done elsewhere, handed down Owned by the team PM's role Project manage delivery Own value and viability Failure looks like Late shipment A metric that didn't move Cagan's word Mercenaries Missionaries

Cagan borrows John Doerr's line: you want missionaries, not mercenaries . Mercenaries build what they are told and go home. They have no stake in whether it works, because they were never trusted with the why . Missionaries understand the problem deeply enough to care whether they solved it, and they will fight for the user and push back on a bad spec. You do not get missionaries by writing "be passionate" in a job description. You get them by giving the team real problems and real autonomy, which is to say by making it a product team. Culture follows structure here, not the other way around.

sections.h: Why this is the PM's fight
sections.t: Here is the part that is personal to the PM job. On a feature team, the PM role collapses into Product Manager vs Adjacent Roles. If the roadmap is handed down and discovery happened upstairs, there is nothing left for you but backlog grooming and delivery coordination. You become a project manager with a product title. Every reason this Atlas exists, including discovery, strategy, the The Four Big Product Risks, and judgment, presupposes a product team. On a feature team, most of your competencies have no surface to act on. So when I tell PMs to push for outcomes rather than features, I am not being philosophical. I am telling them to defend the conditions under which their job is worth doing.

sections.h: Most teams are feature teams in disguise
sections.t: Here is the uncomfortable part. Most teams that call themselves product teams are feature teams wearing the vocabulary. They say "outcomes" and then measure velocity. They run discovery theater, a few interviews to justify a decision already made. The tells are easy to spot once you know them. [!warning] You're on a feature team if… Your Building and Managing a Roadmap is a list of features with dates, not problems with target metrics. Success is defined as "shipped," and nobody checks the outcome 60 days later. Discovery, if it happens, happens after the decision, to build the case. Leadership hands down solutions ("build a referral program"), not problems ("activation is too low"). The fastest way to get praised is to ship a lot, regardless of whether anything moved. If you recognize your team here, that is not cause for despair. It is the diagnosis. The move is to quietly start attaching an outcome metric to everything you ship and reporting on it, whether or not anyone asked. That is how you convert a feature team one decision at a time. The structural version of this choice, owner versus facilitator and outcome versus feature, is laid out in The Product Organization Matrix.

The Product Teams vs Feature Teams distinction tells you how the team is run . But it leaves out a second variable that changes the PM job just as much: how much authority you actually have over the decision. Stack those two axes and you get a 2x2 I keep coming back to, the Product Organization Matrix. It locates your real situation more precisely than any job title, and it explains why two people with "Senior PM" on their badge can be doing completely different jobs.

[!note] The axes Team type (horizontal): Outcome to Feature. Is your team handed problems and outcomes (empowered) or handed a roadmap of features to deliver? This is Cagan's distinction. PM posture (vertical): Owner to Facilitator. Are you accountable for the decision (owner), or do you bring the right people together so they decide (facilitator)? Owners decide and answer for it. Facilitators orchestrate and align.

Outcome team Feature team PM as Owner The empowered PM. You own a problem and the call on how to solve it. The job in full: discovery, Understanding Trade offs\, outcomes. Cagan's ideal. The benevolent dictator. You own decisions but the team executes a fixed roadmap. Common in Product Management Across Company Stages\ founder led products. Fast, fragile, does not scale. PM as Facilitator The coordinator. Outcomes are the goal but you align rather than decide. Typical when authority sits with a strong design or eng lead, or in a Cross Functional Collaboration\ of equals. Healthy if the team is senior, a vacuum if it is not. The order taker. Fixed roadmap, no decision authority. You are a Product Manager vs Adjacent Roles\ with a product title. Avoid building a career here. The diagonal from bottom right to top left is the growth path: order taker, then toward owning either the decision or the outcome, then toward owning both. The top left is where the PM role is most fully itself.

sections.h: Owner vs Facilitator is a real choice, not a failing
sections.t: I want to be careful here, because "facilitator" can sound like the loser quadrant and it is not. Facilitation is the right posture sometimes. When your team is genuinely senior, say a staff engineer and a principal designer who have each forgotten more than you know about their craft, the best thing you can do is facilitate. Frame the problem, bring the right context, and let the experts decide, stepping in only to own The Four Big Product Risks. Forcing yourself into "owner" of an interaction design call you are unqualified to make is how PMs lose the room. The failure mode is not being a facilitator. It is being a facilitator by default , defaulting to consensus because you are avoiding the accountability of a decision. An empowered team with a facilitator who will not own value and viability has a hole at the center of the The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn. [!tip] How to use the matrix Locate yourself honestly, then ask what move the situation needs. In order taker , the move is to attach outcome metrics to your work until the team drifts left. In coordinator , the move is to make sure someone owns value and viability, ideally you. In benevolent dictator , the move is to start trusting the team with problems so it survives your absence. The matrix is not a verdict. It is a compass.

The horizontal axis is Cagan's outcome vs feature distinction directly. The vertical axis adds the lived reality that even on an empowered team, how much you personally decide varies with your seniority, your team's seniority, and your org's culture. Together they explain a thing titles cannot: why the job feels so different from company to company even at the same level. Your title is set by The Product Career Ladder. Your actual job is set by your quadrant.

"Product manager" is one title covering jobs so different that practitioners often cannot do each other's work. A growth PM optimizing a checkout funnel and a platform PM designing an internal API share a competency model and almost nothing else day to day. I think the single biggest mistake early career PMs make is assuming there is one PM job to master. There is not. There is a core craft, the The PM Competency Model, expressed through wildly different specializations. Knowing which one you are in, and which one a job posting is actually for, saves you years.

[!note] The types I'd name Core or feature PM. Owns a product area in a customer facing app, the default mental image of the job. Broadest competency demand. Growth PM. Owns a metric (activation, retention, conversion) rather than a feature area. Lives in funnels, Experimentation and A B Testing, and Product Analytics and Metrics. Heaviest Fluency with Data demand of any type. Platform or technical PM. Customers are internal teams or external developers , and deliverables are APIs, SDKs, and infrastructure. Heaviest feasibility and architecture fluency, lightest on consumer UX. 0 to 1 PM. Finds product market fit from nothing. All discovery and judgment, little optimization. Tolerance for ambiguity is the whole job. Scaling or 1 to n PM. Takes a product that works and makes it work at 100x. Optimization, reliability, and The Product Organization Matrix design over invention. AI PM. Builds products on probabilistic models. A genuinely different craft, and it gets its own treatment in The AI Product Manager. These are not mutually exclusive. A growth PM on an AI product at a scaleup is all three at once. They are lenses, not boxes.

Cutting across all of the above is the axis I know best, having spent my career in B2C mobile (sports, media, betting): B2C B2B User = buyer? Usually yes Usually no; buyer, user, and admin differ Decision driver Emotion, habit, delight ROI, workflow fit, procurement Volume Millions of low touch users Fewer, higher value accounts Discovery Product Analytics and Metrics\ plus broad research Deep User Research and Discovery\, sales calls, advisory boards Killer competency User Experience Design\ and retention instinct Stakeholder Management\ and viability management Here is why this matters. A B2C PM who is brilliant at funnel optimization can flounder in B2B, because the user cannot tell you what they need over an analytics dashboard. You have to go talk to them, and the person who pays is not the person who clicks. Neither is harder. They are different. Do not assume your instincts transfer.

Underneath every type, the The Four Big Product Risks and the Understanding Trade offs act are invariant. Every PM, in every specialization, is managing value, usability, feasibility, and viability. Only the weights change. A platform PM weights feasibility heavily and usability lightly. A growth PM weights value, measured as conversion, above all. The competencies are the same alphabet. The specializations are different words. [!warning] The myth of the universal PM Job descriptions love "we need a strong generalist PM." Real teams need a specific shape for a specific problem (see The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape). When you interview, figure out which type the role actually is, because the title will not tell you. Ask what the one metric or outcome is that this PM owns. The answer reveals the type faster than any job description.

title: The T-Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape

sections.t: The most freeing idea in my career was learning that I did not have to be good at everything. The job description implies a superhuman: strategist, designer, data scientist, diplomat, engineer. Nobody is all of those. The great PMs I know are not well rounded. They are spiky , exceptional at two or three things and merely adequate at the rest, and they are ruthlessly honest about which is which. Knowing your shape, and building a team that covers your flats, beats pretending to be uniformly excellent every time.

The T shaped metaphor comes from IDEO and Tim Brown, with roots in a 1991 Independent article. The vertical bar is depth in one discipline, and the horizontal bar is breadth across many. A T shaped PM has deep expertise in something, say Fluency with Data or User Experience Design, and enough literacy across engineering, design, strategy, and research to collaborate credibly with specialists. Depth earns you respect. Breadth lets you span the The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn. You need both bars. Pure breadth with no depth is the dreaded jack of all trades and master of none, where you can talk to everyone and impress no one.

Ravi Mehta sharpened this into what I think is the truer picture. Most PMs are not T shaped. They are spiky , with multiple peaks and real valleys rather than one tidy vertical bar. His key insight is that great products are built by spiky people on well rounded teams. You do not fix a valley by grinding it up to average. You fix it by pairing with someone whose spike is your valley. [!quote] Mehta, on shape The goal is not to become uniformly strong across the competency model. The goal is to be exceptional where it counts and to compose a team that is well rounded, even though no individual on it is. Mehta also names two shapes worth knowing. Product Builders are spiky toward execution and customer insight, and they make things. Product Architects are spiky toward strategy, and they shape direction. Most people lean one way. Neither is better. They are suited to different problems and different Types of Product Managers of PM role.

Self perception is unreliable here, because we overrate our spikes and rationalize our valleys. So triangulate. [!tip] Three ways to map your shape 1. Score yourself, then get scored. Run The Competency Self Assessment across the twelve competencies, then have a manager or peer score you blind. The gaps between the two are where your self image is wrong, usually in both directions. 2. Look at where work feels effortless versus grinding. Your spikes are the things you do well without it feeling like effort. Genuine grind, even after practice, is a flat. Effort spent is a better signal than outcome achieved. 3. Ask what people come to you for. Your reputation is the market's read on your shape, and it is often more accurate than your own.

sections.h: Build the team around the shape
sections.t: Once you know your shape, the implication is direct, and it changes how you hire (Hiring and Building a Product Team). Hire and partner for your valleys, not clones of your spikes. A strategy spiky PM who hires another strategist has built a team that cannot ship. The strongest pods I have run paired a builder shaped PM with an architect shaped manager, or a data spiky PM with a design spiky lead. Complementarity beats redundancy. [!warning] The well rounded myth "Develop into a well rounded PM" is common career advice, and I think it is mostly wrong. Rounding off your spikes to raise your valleys makes you less valuable, not more, because you trade a rare strength for common adequacy. Invest in your spikes until they are undeniable. Invest in your valleys only to the threshold of "won't embarrass me in the room." The exception is a valley that is blocking your level. See Identifying Growth Areas for when a flat actually has to be raised.

The same competencies, the same title, the same person, and a completely different job depending on the size and stage of the company. I have watched brilliant startup PMs stall at an enterprise and seasoned enterprise PMs freeze at a seed stage company. Neither lost their skill. They failed to re read the room. Company stage changes which competencies matter most, how much authority you hold, and what "good" even means. If you are choosing a job or struggling in one, this is often the missing variable.

[!note] What the job optimizes for Startup (seed to Series A, pre PMF): survival. Find Crafting a Product Vision before the money runs out. Speed and learning over process and polish. Scaleup (Series B to D, post PMF): growth and the transition from chaos to system. Make a thing that works work at 100x without breaking it. The hardest stage to PM in. Enterprise (public or late stage): leverage and protection. Move a huge base safely, defend the moat, coordinate across many teams and stakeholders.

Dimension Startup Scaleup Enterprise Core risk Value (will anyone want it?) Feasibility and viability at scale Viability, brand, regulatory PM count 0 to 2, often the founder Growing fast, first process Many, with a clear ladder Authority High but informal, you just do it Negotiated, roles forming Lower per person, spread across teams Process Almost none, and that's correct Being invented (the painful part) Heavy, and you work through it Hottest competency User Research and Discovery\ and Product Sense and Judgment\ Product Strategy\ and Stakeholder Management\ Managing Up\ and Executive Communication\ Failure mode Building what nobody wants Process that strangles speed, or none that allows scale Politics, shipping nothing safely

The danger is not lack of skill. It is applying the right instinct in the wrong stage. [!warning] The two classic mismatches Enterprise PM at a startup. Reaches for process, alignment meetings, and thorough documentation in a place that needs a shipped prototype by Friday. Mistakes rigor for value and dies of slowness. At pre PMF, The 10 50 90 Execution Framework are usually premature precision. Startup PM at an enterprise. "Just ship it" runs straight into legal, brand, a 40 million user base, and six dependent teams. Mistakes speed for progress and gets a reputation for recklessness. The blast radius of a bad call is thousands of times larger. Neither person is wrong about how to PM . They are wrong about where they are . The fix is humility about stage, not a new skill set.

sections.h: The stage shapes the team type too
sections.t: Stage and The Product Organization Matrix interact. Startups are often a single Product Teams vs Feature Teams by default, because there is no one to hand down a roadmap, so the team owns the problem whether it wants to or not. Enterprises drift toward Product Teams vs Feature Teams because coordination cost makes top down roadmapping tempting, and that drift is exactly what the best enterprise product leaders fight. Knowing your stage tells you which gravity you are fighting.

sections.h: What this means for your career
sections.t: If you are deciding where to go next, match the stage to your The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape. Spiky on discovery and judgment with high ambiguity tolerance? Startups will love you. Spiky on stakeholder management and execution at scale? You will thrive at a scaleup or enterprise where a startup would bore you. And know that the senior ladder (Director and VP of Product, The Chief Product Officer) is a different animal at each stage. A VP at a 30 person startup and a VP at a public company share a title and little else.

When I assess a PM, whether to hire, to coach, or to promote, I sort everything they bring into a spectrum from hard to teach to easy to teach. It is the most practical mental model I have for developing people, because it tells you where to spend coaching energy and, more importantly, what to screen for in hiring that you will never be able to install later. The headline is simple. Hire for the things you cannot teach, train the things you can, and do not confuse the two.

This is one of my own frameworks, Skill Coaching × Effort, and it lays out five layers, hardest to teach on the left, easiest on the right: Layer Examples How teachable Where you address it Character traits Humility, empathy, integrity, growth mindset, decisiveness, resilience, values Barely; set early, change slowly Hire for it. Screen hard. Competencies Leadership, communication, collaboration, execution, critical thinking, analysis, creativity, strategy Slowly, with real effort over years Coach + experience (The Six Stages of Mastery) Techniques User stories, backlog management, research methods, data viz, working with eng, SQL Months, with practice Coach + reps Processes Scrum, Kanban, OKRs, JTBD, Kano, Design Sprint Weeks Train + a good doc Tools Jira, Amplitude, Figma, Optimizely, Intercom Days Train. Almost free. Read it right and the strategic point jumps out. The further left, the more it determines a PM's ceiling, and the less you can do about it after you hire. Tools and processes are trivially teachable, so they should carry almost no weight in a hiring decision, yet job descriptions are stuffed with them ("must know Jira, Amplitude, Mixpanel"). Character is nearly unteachable and determines everything, yet it is the hardest to assess in an interview, so it gets underweighted. That inversion is most hiring mistakes in one sentence.

Marty Cagan compresses the whole left side into a phrase I have never improved on: he staffs teams with competent people of character . Two words, both load bearing. [!quote] Cagan's bar Competence is necessary, because a PM who cannot do the job is a liability no matter how lovely. But competence without character is worse, because a capable person who lacks integrity, humility, or empathy will use their capability to win arguments, hide problems, and protect themselves over the user. Character is not a nice to have on top of skill. It is the thing that decides whether the skill points the right way. In my experience the most dangerous hire is not the under skilled one. That is visible and fixable. It is the highly competent one with a character flaw: the brilliant PM who is quietly contemptuous of engineers, or cannot say "I was wrong," or shades the data to look good. Their competence makes the damage bigger and harder to catch.

[!tip] How I use the spectrum Hiring: spend your hardest scrutiny on the left. Probe for humility ("tell me about a time you were wrong"), empathy (how they talk about past teams), and growth mindset (what they have learned recently). Assume you can teach Jira. See The PM Interview and Hiring Guide. Coaching: target competencies and techniques, the middle, where effort actually moves the needle. You cannot coach someone into having character, but you can build their Communication or Fluency with Data over a year. That is where The Manager as Coach earns its keep. Don't fake it. You cannot coach a character flaw away with feedback. If someone's core issue is integrity or humility, no amount of Coaching and Feedback fixes it, and pretending otherwise wastes everyone's time. That is a hiring miss to correct, not a development plan.

sections.h: The myth of the well rounded résumé
sections.t: Here is the flip side. Do not reject a candidate because they have not used your stack. A PM of genuine character and strong competencies will learn your tools in a week. A PM with a perfect tool list and a brittle character will sink your team in a quarter. Hire left, train right. And note that character plus competency sits beneath The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape. Shape tells you which competencies someone has. This spectrum tells you which of their gaps you can actually do something about.

Product sense is the competency nobody can fully define and everybody can recognize. It is why one PM looks at the same research as everyone else and proposes the thing that works , while another proposes something reasonable that lands flat. For a long time the field treated it as innate, as if you either have taste or you do not. I think that is both wrong and lazy. Product sense is trainable , it is one of the most valuable skills a PM can build, and the PMs who improve fastest are the ones who stop treating it as magic.

The cleanest definition I have found comes from Lenny Rachitsky, who ranks the core PM skills in order: communication first, execution second, and product sense third. So this is the third most important skill on his list, and his definition is the one I use. Product sense is the ability to consistently come up with product solutions that meet the needs of the people you are building for. Two words in that sentence carry the weight: consistently and needs . Consistently is the operative word. Anyone can be right once, and that is luck. Product sense is being right more often than chance , and being able to say why before the outcome proves you so. Needs , not wants. Real product sense reads the deep need under the stated want, which is why it is downstream of Jobs To Be Done and Voice of the Customer, not a substitute for them. I would add a third element Lenny emphasizes: product sense is empathy plus creativity . Empathy to genuinely inhabit the user's situation, creativity to generate solutions that fit it. Neither alone is enough.

sections.h: Taste, and the thing beyond data
sections.t: There is a part of product sense that data cannot give you, and I will not pretend otherwise. That part is taste . It is the judgment that this interaction feels right and that one feels cheap, that this is the elegant version and that is the over engineered one. Taste is what is left to decide after the data has narrowed the field but before the answer is obvious, and in my experience that gap is where most product decisions actually live. theScore's principle of balancing game sense with data names this directly: quant and qual and intuition together, never analysis paralysis, never gut alone. Data tells you what happened . Product sense tells you what to do about it. [!warning] The two failure modes Gut only. "I just know what's good." Sometimes true, usually ego, and unfalsifiable, which makes it uncoachable and dangerous at scale. If you cannot articulate why , you do not have product sense. You have an opinion. Data only. Refuses to move without a number, so it only ever optimizes what already exists and never invents. Data cannot tell you what to build next when there is no data on a thing that does not exist yet. That is a judgment call, and dodging it is not rigor. It is abdication. Real product sense lives between these, using data to inform a judgment it is still willing to own .

Because it is trainable, here is how I actually build it, in myself and the PMs I coach: [!tip] Building product sense 1. Make predictions and write them down. Before a launch or experiment, predict the outcome and your reasoning , then check yourself after. This is the fastest loop, because it turns vague intuition into a calibrated, falsifiable skill. (Hypothesis Driven Development is the formal version.) 2. Do teardowns. Take a product you admire and one you don't, and articulate why down to the decision. Borrowed taste becomes your own once you can name the reasons. 3. Increase your at bats with users. Product sense is pattern recognition, and patterns come from volume. Lenny's advice is blunt: talk to more users, see more products, ship more things. 4. Seek the disconfirming case. Hunt for why you might be wrong. The best judgment PMs are the least attached to being right and the most attached to being calibrated.

Product sense becomes more valuable as AI commoditizes the mechanical parts of the job. When AI can draft the spec, run the analysis, and generate ten options, the scarce skill is knowing which option is right . As Reforge puts it, the premium on judgment and taste has never been higher. For probabilistic models specifically, product sense extends into a new dimension covered in The PM's AI Literacy and The AI Product Manager: knowing what a model can reliably do, which is its own kind of taste.

If you internalize one framework from this entire domain, make it this one. Marty Cagan's four big product risks are the cleanest model I know of what a product team is actually managing , and, crucially, who owns what . Every product that fails, fails on at least one of these four. Every product decision is a bet across all four. And the reason the PM job exists at all is that someone has to hold the two risks no one else is accountable for. Cagan's framing, my conviction.

[!quote] Cagan's four big risks Before you build anything, you face four questions. Most teams answer one and ship, then discover the hard way which of the other three killed them. Risk The question Owner Killed by Value Will customers want it, enough to buy, switch, or keep using it? PM Building something nobody needs Usability Can users figure out how to use it? Design A great idea no one can operate Feasibility Can we build it, with the time, skills, and tech we have? Engineering (tech lead) Overpromising what's buildable Viability Does it work for our business , across margin, legal, brand, sales, and partners? PM A product users love that the business can't sustain

sections.h: Who owns what, and why it's the PM job
sections.t: Look at the ownership column. Usability is design's. Feasibility is engineering's. Value and viability are the PM's, and that pairing is the product management job in one line. It is not arbitrary. Design has the craft to judge usability. Engineering has the expertise to judge feasibility. You do not overrule either (The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn). But value and viability require someone with the full context , meaning user insight, market, business model, and GTM, and that is the only seat with all of it. So they fall to you. [!note] Value is the most skipped risk In my experience, value is the risk teams most reliably under test. It is the hardest to assess, because you cannot ask users if they will want something they have never seen and trust the answer, and it is the most tempting to assume ("of course they'll want it"). Continuous Discovery exists almost entirely to attack value risk before you spend engineering's time on feasibility. Skipping value validation is how you build a beautifully engineered, perfectly usable product that nobody uses. [!note] Viability is the most forgotten risk Viability is invisible until it bites: the feature that violates a regulation, torches your unit economics, confuses your brand, undercuts sales, or breaks a partner deal. A PM who only fights for the user and ignores viability is not a user champion. They are half doing the job. This is where Business Acumen and Models and the The DHM Model's margin enhancing lens live. Loving the user and protecting the business is the trade off only you can make.

sections.h: Test risks before you build, not after
sections.t: The deepest point in Cagan's framing is not the taxonomy. It is the sequencing . Address these risks in discovery, before you commit to delivery. The classic, expensive failure is treating all four as build time problems: you spec it, build it, ship it, and only then learn it had no value or was not viable. By then you have spent your most expensive resource, engineering time, to learn what a prototype or a few interviews could have told you in a week. This is the whole logic behind separating discovery from delivery while keeping them on one team (see Product Teams vs Feature Teams). [!tip] How to use the four risks On any meaningful initiative, before building, ask all four out loud and assign each an owner and an evidence level: do we know, or are we assuming? The risk you are assuming hardest, with the least evidence, is where your discovery should go first. This turns a vague "let's de risk it" into a specific, ownable plan, and it dovetails with Risk Identification and Mitigation for the execution phase risks that come after these four.

sections.h: AI adds dimensions, not new risks
sections.t: For AI products, the four risks still hold, because value, usability, feasibility, and viability are timeless. But feasibility and viability acquire sharp new edges. Can the model do this reliably? That is a feasibility question with a probabilistic answer. Does the inference economics work? That is a viability question SaaS never had to ask. More in Building AI Products.

[!quote] The definition I use Product Execution: Define, build, and launch exceptional products. This is the first of the four competency areas in The PM Competency Model, and I put it first on purpose. Execution is the foundation the rest of the PM career sits on. Strategy, vision, and influence stay theoretical until something ships and moves a number. I have watched brilliant strategists wash out because they could not get anything across the line, and I have watched relentless executors get promoted right past them. The order here is not an accident.

sections.h: My point of view: execution is the one thing a PM can never be bad at
sections.t: You can be spiky everywhere else, and you should be. The The PM Competency Model is explicit about it. Nobody is great at all twelve competencies, and chasing a flat profile is how you end up mediocre at everything (The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape). Execution is the one exception I will fight for. A PM who cannot define a clear spec, ship to plan, and hold a quality bar is not a junior PM. They are someone who is not yet doing the job. Everything else multiplies a base of can this person reliably get good product out the door. If the base is zero, the multiplier does not matter. That is also why execution dominates the early ladder. At the Associate Product Manager and Product Manager levels you are largely judged on execution: can you spec a feature, work with engineering, and ship it without it falling over. Strategy and influence are where careers mature (The Product Career Ladder), but they get built on top of an execution reputation you earn in years one through four.

This area breaks into three competencies, each with its own note and its own named behaviors from my model. Competency What it covers The behaviors Feature Specification Turning a problem into shared, buildable understanding Outcome Oriented · Comprehensive Documentation · Clarity Product Delivery Working with the team to actually ship Team Coordination & Alignment · Communication · Delivery to Plan Product Quality Holding the bar on decisions and risk Sound Product Decision Making · Risk Identification & Mitigation

The competencies are the what you are measured on. The rest of this domain is the how I actually do it, the craft and frameworks and beliefs underneath. Note Why it's here Writing PRDs and Specs The craft of the spec: formats, the one pager, over spec vs under spec Agile Delivery and Team Cadence Sprints, standups, and the PM's real role (you are not the scrum master) The 10 50 90 Execution Framework My core execution operating system: phases, confidence, and roles RACI and Decision Rights Who actually decides; Owner/Partner/Consulted; disagree and commit Sound Product Decision Making Reversible vs irreversible doors, decisiveness, owning the call Risk Identification and Mitigation Classify by impact × likelihood, contingency, transparent comms Shipping and Launch Rollout strategy, phased and dark launch, launch readiness, measurement Outcomes Over Outputs My core belief: measure what changed, not what shipped

Execution does not live in a vacuum. Good Feature Specification are downstream of good Continuous Discovery, because you cannot define what to build until you understand the problem and the The Four Big Product Risks. What you choose to execute on is a Prioritization Frameworks decision. And the whole point of shipping is to move a Business Outcome Ownership, which is why Outcomes Over Outputs sits at the end of this domain rather than the start of the strategy one. [!warning] The trap at the top Senior PMs often try to "graduate" out of execution into pure strategy. The best leaders I have worked with never do. They stop writing the spec themselves and start raising the team's execution bar with better processes, better reviews, and fewer surprises. The work changes altitude. It never stops being your responsibility.

[!quote] The definition I use The ability for a PM to gather requirements, define functionality, and set goals in a clear, actionable format that can be used to communicate with the team and drive product delivery. This is the first competency in Product Execution, and it is where the job begins. It is also the thing I coach people on most often, so let me get the framing right before anything else.

A spec is not the document. The document is just the artifact. The spec is the shared understanding it creates. I have watched a beautiful twelve page PRD get ignored by everyone on the team, and I have watched a three sentence Slack message align that same team in an afternoon. The short message did the job. The PRD did not. What you are really producing is alignment: engineers know what to build, design knows the problem, stakeholders know what is coming, and everyone agrees on what "done" and "good" mean. If you wrote a perfect document and the team still does not share that picture, you have not spec'd anything yet. You have filed paperwork. I have done it, and it is a humbling way to learn the difference. The craft of producing the document, the formats and the one pager and the structure, lives in Writing PRDs and Specs. This note is about the competency underneath it: the thinking a good spec makes visible.

I break Feature Specification into three behaviors. [!note] Outcome Oriented Lead with the value, not the implementation, and keep the "why" in front of the reader the whole way through. A spec that opens with a feature list is a tiny feature factory. A good one opens with the problem and the outcome you expect to move, and every requirement ladders back to it. When an engineer asks why you are building it this way, the answer should already be on the page. This is Outcomes Over Outputs applied at the smallest unit of work. [!note] Comprehensive Documentation Cover the problem, the objective, the scope (user stories and acceptance criteria), the UX and technical considerations, and the success metrics. Then cover the things people forget: edge cases, data and instrumentation, risks, and the go to market. Instrumentation is the one I push hardest on. If you did not say how you will measure it, you have quietly decided you will never know whether it worked. Settle that with Product Analytics and Metrics before a line of code gets written. [!note] Clarity Write it so it is easy to read and easy to align on. Clarity is a forcing function for thinking, and a vague spec is usually a sign the thinking is not finished and the PM is hoping the team will close the gap. They will close it. Just not the way you wanted.

sections.h: How it shows up by level
sections.t: The skill does not change as you climb. The scope and the altitude do (see What Changes at Each Level). Level What good looks like Associate Product Manager\ Writes a clear spec for a minor feature, contributes to major ones, gathers what the immediate team needs, and sets a clear, achievable goal. Product Manager\ Specs major, complex functionality on their own, finds the right level of detail without over or under specifying, uses comps and prototypes, and gets feedback at each phase. Senior Product Manager\ Sequences several interrelated specs, considers every device and point of sale even when the launch targets a subset, and folds effort estimates into the requirements. Staff and Principal Product Manager\ Reviews the team's specs, coaches them to clarity, and improves the specification process itself. Director and VP of Product\ Owns how the whole org writes and reviews specs, both the process to create them and the process to give feedback on them.

A few things I actually ask people to try: Write the success metric first. Before you describe the feature, state the number you expect to move. If you cannot name it, you have a discovery problem, not a spec problem, and Continuous Discovery is where to go next. Read the spec back to your engineers and ask them to explain the feature to you. Every gap in their explanation is a gap in your spec. It is the fastest feedback loop I know of. Watch the urge to over specify. A lot of us do it to feel in control, and we end up dictating implementation to engineers who understand the how far better than we do. Own the what and the why. Leave the how to the people building it. Steal formats, not templates. A template is a checklist. A format is a way of thinking. Learn a few of them in Writing PRDs and Specs and match the format to the risk in front of you.

Drafting a PRD is close to free now, so the mechanical writing is no longer where your value sits. What AI cannot do for you is decide which problem is worth solving, choose the outcome, and rule things out of scope. The bottleneck was never the typing. It was the thinking, and that is the part AI quietly hands back to you. Spend the time you save there rather than on a longer document. See Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates.

This is the craft note for Feature Specification. The competency note argues that a spec is shared understanding , not a document. This one is about the document, because the document is still the tool you use to create that understanding, and writing a good one is the thing I coach people on most.

sections.h: My one rule: match the format to the risk
sections.t: There is no single correct spec format, and anyone who tells you there is has not shipped enough different things. A copy change does not need a PRD. A new payments flow needs more than a Slack message. The amount of spec should scale with the The Four Big Product Risks of what you are building. High risk, irreversible, multi team work gets a real document. A Sound Product Decision Making gets a paragraph. The skill the Senior Product Manager level explicitly rewards is finding the right level of detail : not over spec, not under spec.

When the work does warrant a full doc, here is the spine I use. It maps directly onto the Comprehensive Documentation behavior. Section What goes here Why it matters Problem & context Who hurts, how much, what we know Anchors everything in the "why" Objective & success metrics The outcome, the number, how you'll measure No metric means you've decided not to learn Scope User stories + acceptance criteria Defines what "done" means Out of scope What you're explicitly not doing Prevents scope creep and protects the timeline UX & technical considerations Flows, comps, constraints, dependencies Surfaces feasibility risk early Edge cases The unhappy paths Where quality goes to die (Product Quality) Risks & open questions What could go wrong, what's undecided Honesty here saves you later Go to market & rollout How it ships and who needs to know See Shipping and Launch [!tip] The "Out of Scope" section is the most underrated Saying what you won't build is more powerful than the feature list. It is where you make trade offs visible, kill gold plating, and give engineering a hard edge to estimate against. A PRD without an out of scope section is a PRD that will grow until the deadline slips.

For most work I default to a one pager rather than a full PRD: problem, objective plus metric, the few user stories that matter, and the open questions. It fits on a screen, people actually read it, and it forces you to prioritize what is worth communicating. Amazon's six pager and the lean one pager are cousins. Both bet that narrative prose exposes fuzzy thinking that a bulleted template hides, and I agree with that bet. If you cannot write the problem in three clear sentences, you do not understand it yet.

[!warning] Over spec The PM dictates implementation detail to engineers who know the system better than they do. It signals distrust, wastes everyone's time, and produces brittle specs that break the moment reality intervenes. Specify the what and the why; let the team own the how. [!warning] Under spec The PM hand waves the hard parts (edge cases, error states, the metric) and lets the team backfill the thinking mid sprint. This is the more common failure and the more expensive one, because the gaps surface as rework, missed acceptance criteria, and launch day surprises that Product Delivery. [!warning] The write once spec Treating the doc as a contract you write and then abandon. A spec is a living artifact through The 10 50 90 Execution Framework, and it should change as you learn. A spec that looks identical at ship as it did at kickoff means you ran the project with your eyes closed.

This is where AI hits hardest in Product Execution. First drafts that took a day now take minutes. ChatPRD is the most used PM specific AI tool for exactly this reason, and tools like Dovetail now generate evidence backed PRDs that cite the research behind each claim. Use them. Just treat AI as a fast intern rather than an author. It is excellent at structure, completeness, and turning your bullet points into prose. It is useless at choosing the right problem or knowing which edge case will actually bite you in production. The "intent is the source of truth" shift in spec driven development means your value is the quality of the intent, not the polish of the prose. See AI in Prototyping and Delivery and Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates.

[!quote] The definition I use The ability to work closely with one's immediate team (engineering, design, etc.) to iteratively and quickly deliver product functionality that accomplishes pre defined goals. This is the second competency in Product Execution. Feature Specification is about defining the right thing. Product Delivery is about getting it built , fast, iteratively, and without drama. It is the most relational of the three execution competencies. You can spec alone. You cannot deliver alone.

Delivery is the muscle that turns a spec into shipped functionality. The keyword in the definition is iteratively and quickly , not "perfectly" and not "exhaustively." A PM who delivers is one engineering and design want to work with, who keeps the work tied to its objective, who makes trade off calls without melodrama, and who hits the plan with minimal surprises. This is where reputations get built. Engineers talk, and a PM known for clean, predictable delivery gets the benefit of the doubt on everything else.

My model breaks Product Delivery into three named behaviors. [!note] Team Coordination & Alignment Efforts tied to objectives; design, engineering, and business involved early ; effective meetings; explaining the "why" behind trade offs. The early involvement point is the one I will not bend on. PMs who throw a finished spec over the wall and then negotiate every change in real time are creating their own delays. Bring the team in during The 10 50 90 Execution Framework and most of the alignment is already done by the time you build. [!note] Communication Clear, concise, empathetic, across formats, with the right method for the moment, tailored to the audience, and with genuine active listening. Delivery lives and dies on this. A good half of what people call "delivery problems" are communication problems wearing a costume. (The full competency lives over in Communication; here it is specifically in service of shipping.) [!note] Delivery to Plan Delivered to requirements, within engineering's estimate, with a clear schedule, rollout, and scope, and minimal surprises. I weight "minimal surprises" the heaviest of all of these. A slightly late launch you flagged three weeks early is fine. An on time launch nobody was warned might slip is luck, not skill. Predictability is the deliverable. See Shipping and Launch.

sections.h: How it shows up by level
sections.t: The skill is constant. What changes is the size of the trade offs and whose work you are enabling (see What Changes at Each Level). Level What good looks like Associate Product Manager\ Builds working relationships with the immediate team; articulates the goal; makes implementation trade offs with the manager to ship on schedule with few bugs. Product Manager\ Negotiates implementation independently; makes the right speed vs quality calls; knows when to escalate ; recommends what delivers max value fastest. Senior Product Manager\ Understands technical and design trade offs deeply; finds creative solutions balancing speed and quality; motivates the team through hard stretches. Staff and Principal Product Manager\ Makes tough trade offs between projects; spots ways to leverage the team better; identifies unmet team needs (a missing skillset, a tooling gap). Director and VP of Product\ Ensures the right processes exist to maximize speed and quality across all teams; proactively fixes execution issues at the org level.

Treat the estimate as engineering's, not yours. Your job is to protect it by holding scope, removing blockers, and shielding the team, not to negotiate it down. The fastest way to lose engineering's trust is to relitigate their estimate as if effort were a matter of willpower. Run fewer, better meetings. "Effective meetings" is in the behavior for a reason. A standup that reads status aloud is a tax. A standup that surfaces blockers is leverage (Agile Delivery and Team Cadence). Over communicate the schedule. The instant you smell a slip, say so. Surprises are the one true delivery sin. Telegraph everything. Ship smaller. "Iteratively and quickly" is a bias toward slicing work thin. The smallest shippable increment de risks the timeline and starts the learning loop sooner.

AI is reshaping delivery from two directions at once. On one side, the mechanical "product owner" backlog administration (writing tickets, status reports, sprint summaries) is the single easiest AI target in the job, and SVPG is blunt that outcome oriented PMs will be in demand while the ticket pushers will not. On the other, AI prototyping ("vibe coding" with v0, Lovable, Replit) lets PMs and designers ship working prototypes themselves, which compresses the spec to build loop and changes what "delivery" even means for early stage work. The relational core, the coordination and trust and reading the room, is exactly the people and glue work that gets more valuable as the mechanical layer thins out. See AI in Prototyping and Delivery.
