The pyramid looks different depending on where you sit. An Associate Product Manager mostly lives at the base: low risk initiatives, autonomy with manager awareness. A Senior Product Manager owns initiatives across the middle and occasionally the top, and is consulted on the org's big bets. A Director and VP of Product is the senior leadership being consulted, so their job is to be present for the apex bets and to deliberately stay out of the base so the teams own it. Reading the pyramid is partly about knowing which tier of decisions belongs to your level.

AI doesn't alter the governance principle, but it shifts where the line sits, and it adds a new high risk category. Routine, low risk work that used to need a human gate (basic prioritization, content tweaks, standard analyses) increasingly runs with AI assistance and lighter oversight, widening the autonomous base. Meanwhile a new class of apex initiatives appears: probabilistic AI systems in high stakes flows carry risks (hallucination, trust, AI Ethics and Governance) that genuinely warrant senior consultation. The judgment that stays human is the risk classification itself , and deciding which initiative belongs at the top is, fittingly, the kind of call that sits near the top of the pyramid.

[!quote] The definition I use Influencing People : Rally people around the team's work. This is the fourth of the four competency areas in The PM Competency Model, and in my experience it is what separates the PMs who do the job from the PMs who move the org. The other three areas, Product Execution, Customer Insight, and Product Strategy, are about figuring out the right thing and getting it built. This area sits on the uncomfortable fact underneath all of them: you cannot do any of it alone, and you cannot make anyone do anything.

sections.h: My point of view: a PM's power is borrowed, never owned
sections.t: Here is the first thing I tell new PMs. You have no authority. You do not own the engineers' time, the designer's calendar, the budget, or the roadmap of any team but the slice you have been handed. Everything you "get done" is done by people who do not report to you, who could ignore you, and who often outrank you. So the job was never to command. It is to make people want to point their effort at the work you believe in. That is why I do not think of influence as one of the things a PM does. It is the whole job, wearing different hats. Discovery is influence, because you are getting users and the team to tell you the truth. Strategy is influence, because you are getting executives to bet on a direction. Execution is influence, because you are getting a cross functional team to move in sync. Take the influence out and what is left is someone writing documents nobody acts on. [!warning] The "CEO of the product" myth This is the framing I push back on most with new PMs. CEOs hire, fire, and direct. A PM does none of that, so you earn followership or you get nothing. I unpack why I reject it in Leading Without Authority and What Is Product Management.

This area breaks into three competencies, each with its own note and named behaviors drawn from my model. There is an asymmetry worth noticing. Two of these are about influencing outward and upward, and they start on day one. The middle one, leadership, does not apply at the junior levels at all. Competency What it covers The behaviors Stakeholder Management Aligning everyone your work touches Internal Stakeholder Influence & Alignment · External Stakeholder Management · Org Based Communications Team Leadership Managing and growing people Coaching & Performance · Team Impact · Product Wide Influence & Leadership · Resource Management & Hiring Managing Up Leveraging senior leaders Strategic Influence & Alignment · Executive Communications [!note] Team Leadership starts late Unlike every other competency, Team Leadership is rated "Not applicable" at the Associate Product Manager and Early PM levels in the matrix I use. It only begins, informally, once you are a Later Product Manager mentoring others. You influence people for years before you ever lead them. I get into that leveling quirk in the note itself.

The competencies are what you are measured on. The rest of this domain is the craft underneath them: the specific skills of moving people. Note Why it's here Internal and External Stakeholders Mapping who you're influencing and how each type differs Communication The skill I rank first for any PM, full stop Executive Communication BLUF, business framing, brevity. Talking to people short on time Storytelling with Data The story is the unit of persuasion; data is the ammunition Leading Without Authority Influence over control, the reality behind the CEO myth Cross Functional Collaboration Building trust with engineering, design, data, and GTM Coaching and Feedback Giving and receiving feedback; the gateway to leadership Hiring and Building a Product Team Sourcing and evaluating for character and competence

Influence is not a separate phase. It runs through every other area. Your Storytelling with Data are only as good as your Fluency with Data. Your stakeholder alignment is enforced through RACI and Decision Rights. The leadership behaviors here flow straight into the coaching domain (The Manager as Coach) and the The Product Career Ladder, because the entire manager track is this area, scaled up.

This is the first competency in Influencing People, and the one I see PMs underrate until it bites them. You can have the right strategy, a clean spec, and a shipping team, and still get blocked, blindsided, or overruled by someone you forgot to bring along.

[!quote] The definition I use Stakeholder Management : The ability to proactively identify stakeholders impacted by the PM's area and to work with them to factor their requirements into product decisions. Two words in there carry the weight. The first is proactively , because the failure mode is always reactive: finding out about a constraint after it is already a crisis. The second is factor in , not obey. The job is not to satisfy every stakeholder. It is to surface their needs early and make a defensible decision that accounts for them. Saying no to a stakeholder, and doing it well, is stakeholder management too.

I break this competency into three named behaviors. Internal Stakeholder Influence & Alignment Building relationships with the people inside your company that your work depends on or affects, through regular touchpoints , not just when you need something. The core moves are to align on goals, priorities, and metrics before the conflict, communicate persuasively and adapt to the audience, and resolve conflict with empathy rather than escalation. The PMs who are good at this rarely seem to be fighting battles, because they have already had the conversation offline. External Stakeholder Management Trusted relationships with the people outside the building: customers, partners, vendors, sometimes regulators. The work is to translate external feedback into product direction without letting the loudest customer set your roadmap, represent the company credibly, manage expectations transparently, and create mutual value. In my B2C mobile world this meant league partners, payment providers, and platform owners like Apple and Google, whose constraints I did not control but had to design around. Org Based Communications Articulating vision, strategy, and priorities at every level of the org, and adapting the message to each one. The same roadmap update lands differently for an engineer, a marketer, and a VP. This behavior leans hard on Storytelling with Data: transparent updates that combine narrative and numbers so people stay aligned without you in the room.

sections.h: How it shows up by level
sections.t: The escalation here is about scope of influence , from one feature out to the whole company. Level What stakeholder management looks like Associate Product Manager\ Identifies the obvious stakeholders for one feature , with manager support; keeps them informed Product Manager\ Independently maps and aligns stakeholders for complex work; runs the touchpoints; handles routine conflict Senior Product Manager\ Owns alignment across multiple teams; trusted to manage high stakes external relationships; resolves real disputes Group Product Manager\ / Director and VP of Product\ Aligns stakeholders at the org level; sets the communication cadence others follow; manages executive tier partners VP+ Builds the company wide norms for how product communicates and aligns; the relationships are the strategy

A few things I actually coach people to do: Make a stakeholder map and keep it current. Start with Internal and External Stakeholders: who is impacted, who decides, who just wants to be informed. Pair it with RACI and Decision Rights so "stakeholder" does not quietly blur into "person who can veto me." Front load the relationship. Schedule the touchpoint before you need the favor. Influence runs on a trust balance you fund in advance. Practice the persuasive update until people act on your Storytelling with Data without needing a meeting. Get good at the no. Acknowledge the need, explain the trade off, offer the alternative. A respected no beats a resented yes, generally.

The mechanical layer here is the first thing AI takes off your plate: drafting the stakeholder update, summarizing the meeting, writing the status report. It is a top example in Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates, and I now generate a first draft update in seconds. What does not commoditize is the judgment about whose requirements to weight, the read on a room, and the trust you have banked. Lenny Rachitsky calls the durable PM core the "people/glue" work, and stakeholder management is the heart of it. AI will write the memo. It cannot have the hard hallway conversation for you. [!warning] Myth: stakeholder management means keeping everyone happy It does not. That is how you get a watered down, design by committee product. The goal is alignment , not universal approval: everyone heard, the trade offs explicit, the decision owned. A PM optimizing for zero unhappy stakeholders is optimizing for the wrong thing.

A stakeholder is anyone whose interests your product touches: whose support you need, whose constraints you inherit, or who can derail you if they are surprised. The first behavior under Stakeholder Management is proactively identifying them, and I take "proactively" literally. You map them before the project, not in the post mortem.

sections.h: The trap of treating them as one blob
sections.t: New PMs say "I need to manage my stakeholders" as if it is a single task. It is not. A sales leader who wants a feature for one deal, a platform owner whose policy you have to comply with, and an engineering manager you share a team with are three completely different relationships. Lump them together and you will over communicate with the people who do not care and under communicate with the ones who will sink you. So I split them on two axes: internal versus external, and how much they can actually affect the outcome.

People inside your company. You will see them in the hallway, share OKRs with them, and need them repeatedly, so the relationship compounds over time. The major types: Type What they want How I manage them Engineering & Design Clear problems, stable scope, the "why" Daily partnership; this is Cross Functional Collaboration, not "stakeholder management" Executives / leadership Confidence the bet is sound; no surprises Managing Up: brevity, business framing, Executive Communication Sales / GTM / Marketing Things to sell; launch dates; messaging Translate their asks into problems; never let one deal set the roadmap Support / Ops Fewer tickets; advance warning of changes A rich Voice of the Customer channel; warn them before launches Data / Legal / Finance Correctness, compliance, viable economics Pull them in early as consultants, not last minute approvers

People outside the building. The stakes per interaction are higher, the contact is less frequent, and you represent the whole company when you talk to them. Customers and users are the most important external voice, but treat their feedback as input, not instruction. (How I separate signal from the loudest request: Voice of the Customer.) Partners , which in my world meant league and media partners and co marketing deals. Mutual value, or the relationship rots. Vendors and platform owners like Apple, Google, and payment processors. You do not control their constraints, so you design around them. Manage expectations transparently and keep your credibility intact.

Identification on its own is not enough. I sort everyone by influence on the outcome and interest in it , the classic 2x2. It tells me where to spend communication effort. Low interest High interest High influence Keep satisfied (execs who must not be surprised) Manage closely (partners, decision makers, your core team) Low influence Monitor (a light update is plenty) Keep informed (engaged users, adjacent teams) The failure mode is inverting it: pouring energy into vocal low influence stakeholders while a high influence one quietly loses confidence in you.

This is where mapping gets sharp. A stakeholder map tells you who cares . It does not tell you who decides . Conflating the two is how PMs end up paralyzed, treating everyone with an opinion as a veto. That is why I always pair the map with RACI and Decision Rights, so every stakeholder gets an explicit role: Owner, Partner/Approver, Consulted, or merely Informed. "You are a consulted stakeholder, not an approver on this one" is one of the most useful sentences a PM can say, and it is only possible once the decision rights are written down. [!tip] Run the map at kickoff Take five minutes at the start of any non trivial initiative: list everyone the work touches, place them in the 2x2, assign each a RACI and Decision Rights role, and decide your cadence for each. It is the cheapest insurance I know against a late stage blindside.

This is the second competency in Influencing People, and the one that behaves differently from all the others. Where Stakeholder Management and Managing Up start on day one, this is the one competency you cannot grow in your first few years. It needs people to lead, and junior PMs do not have any.

[!quote] The definition I use Team Leadership : The ability to manage and mentor direct reports so they deliver on their product areas, improve against these competencies, deliver outcomes, and achieve career objectives. Notice the object: direct reports . This is the line where "influence" turns into "management." It carries a duty the other competencies do not, because you are now accountable not just for outcomes but for people and their careers. This is the spine of the entire manager track on the career ladder.

Four named behaviors sit under this competency. Coaching & Performance Actionable feedback through regular 1:1s, tailored to each person's strengths and goals, modeling strong practices yourself, and creating a safe space to learn. This is the heart of the job. Cagan calls coaching a manager's number one responsibility, worth roughly half their time. It flows into Coaching and Feedback and The Manager as Coach. Team Impact Aligning the team's goals to the business, holding accountability for outcomes, not activity , and removing blockers so the team can move. The shift here is profound. You stop being measured on what you ship and start being measured on what your team achieves (Outcomes Over Outputs). Product Wide Influence & Leadership Being a company wide advocate for the product discipline: improving processes, tools, and standards, investing in development and hiring, and fostering a healthy product culture. This is where a leader's impact stops being contained to their own team. Resource Management & Hiring Defining role expectations, attracting diverse high potential candidates, evaluating holistically, making inclusive evidence based decisions, and aligning org structure to the work. The detailed craft lives in Hiring and Building a Product Team.

sections.h: How it shows up by level
sections.t: [!warning] The leveling quirk: N/A until you lead In the matrix I use, Team Leadership is scored "Not applicable" at the Associate Product Manager\ and Early PM levels. You can be a fantastic individual PM with this competency at zero, and that is fine, because there is no one to lead yet. It is the clearest proof that the competency model is leveled: the same twelve competencies, but the bar, and even the relevance, changes with altitude. Level What Team Leadership looks like Associate Product Manager\ / Early PM Not applicable. No reports; influence happens through the other competencies Later Product Manager\ Begins informally : mentoring an APM, modeling practices, no formal authority yet Senior Product Manager\ Coaches juniors seriously; may have a dotted line report; owns team outcomes Group Product Manager\ First true people management. Direct reports, performance, hiring; runs the operating rhythm Director and VP of Product\ Leads managers of managers; sets product culture, processes, and the org's hiring bar company wide [!note] This is where the ladder forks Up to Senior Product Manager, everyone grows the same way. After that, the manager track is defined by maxing this competency, while the IC track (Staff, Principal) scales influence and ambiguity without taking on reports. Choosing between them is a real decision, not a default. See The IC vs Manager Decision.

Before you have reports, mentor. As a Later PM, take an APM under your wing. Practice the coaching muscle before it is your job. Separate the two halves. Cagan splits leadership into management (coaching and staffing) and leadership (vision and strategic context). Both matter, and they are different skills. Measure the team, not yourself. This is the hardest reflex to unlearn. Stop optimizing your own output and start removing your team's blockers (Outcomes Over Outputs). Run the leveling honestly. Use the same model on your reports that was used on you, and turn the gaps into a real plan (Building a Growth Plan).

This is the competency AI touches least, and it is worth being clear about why. A model can draft a performance review, summarize a 1:1, or surface team health signals, which is real mechanical leverage. What it cannot do is coach a struggling PM through a hard quarter, earn a team's trust, make a difficult people call, or build a culture. The durable core is judgment, taste, and the kind of leadership people choose to follow (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates). If anything, AI raises the premium here. As the mechanical PM work gets automated, leading the humans who do the irreducible work becomes a larger share of what a senior leader is actually for.

This is the third competency in Influencing People, and the one a lot of ambitious PMs are quietly bad at. We get trained to manage down and across. Managing up feels like politics, so people avoid it, and that is a mistake. Your manager and the executives above you are your most powerful lever, and learning to use them well is not careerism. It is the job.

[!quote] The definition I use Managing Up : The ability to leverage senior managers/executives to help achieve goals, deliver outcomes, and positively influence the strategic direction of the team and company. The operative word is leverage . Executives have what you do not: budget, headcount, air cover, and the ability to unblock things across the org with one sentence. Managing up is the discipline of giving them what they need (clarity, confidence, early warning) so they give you what you need. It runs in both directions, because you also influence their thinking and shape strategy from below.

Two named behaviors sit under this competency. Strategic Influence & Alignment Connecting your product vision to the company's strategy, and using rationale, storytelling, and data to influence the people who set direction. The skilled moves are to navigate ambiguity and competing executive interests, champion shared outcomes over your own turf, and build genuine trust with senior functions. This is how a PM's idea becomes a company bet, by being framed in the language of the company's goals rather than the team's preferences. Executive Communications This is a craft specific enough that I gave it its own note: Executive Communication. The essence is to be concise and focused on strategic impact, framed in business terms tied to KPIs and ROI, data backed, with questions and risks already handled, and delivered calm and solution oriented. Executives are chronically short on time and long on context, and communication that ignores both will fail no matter how good the underlying work is.

sections.h: How it shows up by level
sections.t: The escalation tracks whose attention you can hold and influence , from your own manager up to the C suite and board. Level What managing up looks like Associate Product Manager\ Keeps their manager informed; flags blockers early; no surprises in the 1:1 Product Manager\ Communicates outcomes upward independently; makes asks for help with clear rationale Senior Product Manager\ Influences leadership on direction; trusted in exec forums; frames work in business terms Group Product Manager\ / Director and VP of Product\ Shapes strategy with executives; manages sideways across peer leaders; owns exec narratives VP+ Is the executive, and now manages up to the CEO and board (The Chief Product Officer) [!tip] Make your manager's job easy The simplest reframe that works: your manager is also managing up. Every clean, well framed update you give them is something they can pass upward with confidence. Be the report they never have to translate, chase, or worry about. That reputation is worth more than any single ask.

Lead with the answer. Adopt BLUF, bottom line up front, in everything you send up. The detail follows the conclusion, it never precedes it. (Executive Communication.) Frame in their currency. Translate "we shipped X" into "this moves the metric they are accountable for" (Business Outcome Ownership, OKRs and Goal Setting). Anticipate the question. Before any exec conversation, write the three hardest questions they will ask and answer them in advance. Half of managing up is doing the executive's thinking for them. Build trust in low stakes moments. Do not let the first real exec interaction be a crisis. Bank credibility through consistent, surprise free delivery.

The mechanical half here is squarely in the zone AI now handles in minutes: turning a messy quarter into a crisp exec summary, drafting the board slide, pulling the KPI deltas (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates). I lean on it for first drafts. The high value half is untouched, though. Knowing which number an executive cares about, reading the currents between competing leaders, and earning the trust that makes influence real are all human work. Think of AI as a fast speechwriter with no standing in the room. The judgment of what to say to whom, and why now, stays with you. [!warning] Myth: managing up is brown nosing The cynical read, that managing up is flattery and self promotion, keeps good PMs from doing it, and that hurts their teams. Done right, it is the opposite of politics. You are giving leadership the clarity to make good decisions and securing the resources your team needs. A PM who refuses to manage up is not principled. They are leaving their team under supported.

If I could keep only one skill for a PM, it would be this one. Not strategy, not product sense, not data. Communication. Everything a PM produces, whether a spec, a roadmap, a decision, or a vision, is inert until it is transmitted into another person's head and changes what they do. A brilliant idea you cannot communicate is functionally identical to no idea at all.

sections.h: My point of view: this is the 1 PM skill
sections.t: I am not alone here. Lenny Rachitsky, after studying what separates great PMs, ranks the skills in order: communication first, execution second, product sense third. Communication sits above execution, not beside it. His logic matches my experience. A PM's leverage is entirely indirect, because you act through other people, so your effectiveness is capped by how clearly you can move information and conviction between minds. Brandon Chu put it even more sharply. The PM is "the API" of the team, the interface every other function calls to coordinate. An API with a garbled contract breaks everything downstream. That is what a PM with poor communication is: a broken interface. [!tip] The test I apply Good PM communication is not measured by how much you said. It is measured by what the listener does next . If your update did not change a priority, unblock a decision, or align a team, it did not communicate. Output is words; outcome is behavior.

These are different skills and most PMs are lopsided. Know which you're weaker at. What it's for Where PMs fail Written Async alignment at scale, durable decisions, the spec, the update Burying the point; writing for themselves, not the reader; length as a proxy for rigor Verbal Real time alignment, persuasion, conflict, reading the room Talking past the point; not listening; mistaking talking for communicating Writing is the higher leverage half, because it scales. One well written doc aligns fifty people you will never meet, and it persists after you leave the room. It is also the more honest half, because muddled writing is a reliable tell for muddled thinking. The named behaviors in my model reflect this: clear, concise, empathetic communication across formats shows up under both Product Delivery and Stakeholder Management.

The single highest leverage habit is the same message, rebuilt for each audience. A roadmap change is not one communication. It is at least four. To engineering , the why behind the trade off, the constraint, the technical context. To design , the user problem and the experience bar. To executives , the business impact, in one line, up front (Executive Communication). To the wider org , the narrative: what is changing, why it matters, what to do (Storytelling with Data). A PM who sends the same generic update to everyone has confused broadcasting with communicating . The information went out; the alignment did not happen. [!warning] Myth: communication means being a good talker The charismatic PM who dominates meetings is often a worse communicator than the quiet one whose docs are crystalline. Communication includes active listening , and the loud ones are frequently the ones not listening. Do not confuse presence with effectiveness.

This is the most interesting tension in the whole domain. AI is a phenomenal writing tool. It drafts the update, tightens the prose, restructures the doc. So one reading is that AI erodes the value of communication. I think the opposite is true. As the Reforge "higher ground" framing argues, when first drafts get cheap, the bottleneck moves to clarity of thought , knowing what you actually want to say and to whom. AI can fix your grammar. It cannot decide your point. Lenny describes PMs becoming editors of super intelligent suggestions, and editing is a communication skill. The medium gets easier while the judgment underneath gets more valuable. (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates.)

A specialized, high stakes branch of Communication and the core craft behind Managing Up. Talking to executives is not talking to your team with bigger words. It is a different game with different rules, and PMs who do not learn them get filtered out, not because their work is bad but because it never lands.

sections.h: The one thing to understand about executives
sections.t: They are short on time and attention, and long on context you lack. They sit across many product areas, pattern match against years of bets, and decide where the next dollar and headcount go. Every norm below falls out of that single fact. Write for someone who has ninety seconds, has seen this movie before, and is asking "so what should I do about it?"

The most important habit, and the one PMs resist hardest. Lead with the conclusion. The ask, the recommendation, the headline number, all in the first sentence. Then the supporting detail, for those who want it. PMs are trained to build an argument: context, then analysis, then conclusion. That structure is for people who will read to the end. Executives will not. They want the answer, and they will pull on the threads they care about. Burying your recommendation under three paragraphs of setup is the single most common way smart PMs lose an executive's attention. [!example] Same content, two openings Buries it: "Over the last quarter we ran three experiments on onboarding, analyzed retention cohorts, consulted with data science, and after reviewing the funnel we believe..." BLUF: "Recommendation: kill the new onboarding flow. It dropped activation 8%. Detail and the three experiments behind it below." The second one respects the reader. The first makes them dig.

Executives think in Business Outcome Ownership, not features. Translate everything into their currency before you walk in: Not "we shipped the referral feature" but "referrals drove 12% of new signups this month." Not "engineering needs two more sprints" but "two more weeks de risks a launch tied to our Q3 OKRs and Goal Setting." Tie it to KPIs, ROI, and the metrics they are accountable for (Strategic Impact). If you cannot state the business impact of your work in one sentence, you are not ready for the exec conversation, and that gap is usually a sign the work lacks a clear outcome, not just the framing.

Be ruthless. One slide where you had planned five. One sentence where you had written a paragraph. Brevity is not dumbing down. It is having done the synthesis for the reader rather than making them do it. A long memo to an executive often signals you have not found the point yourself.

The behavior that separates senior communicators from junior ones. Before any exec interaction, I write down the three hardest questions they could ask, the skeptical ones, the "what about cost," the "why not the cheaper option," and I have crisp answers ready. There are two payoffs. I am never caught flat, and I often pre empt the question entirely by addressing it in the appendix. Half of Managing Up is doing the executive's thinking for them. [!tip] Calm and solution oriented When something is on fire, the executive is reading you as much as the data. Lead with the situation, the plan, and what you need, not anxiety. "Here is what broke, here is what we are doing, here is the one decision I need from you" beats a panicked data dump every time. Composure under pressure is itself a credibility signal.

Executive comms is mechanically AI friendly. Drafting the board slide, compressing a quarter into three bullets, restructuring rambling notes into BLUF order, all of it is minutes of work now (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates). I use it as a first pass editor routinely. What it cannot do is the judgment underneath: which metric this particular executive cares about, which risk is worth surfacing, and how much candor the moment calls for. The slide is the easy part. Knowing what belongs on it, and what the executive will actually do with it, stays a human read.

This is the persuasion engine of the whole Influencing People area. It shows up by name in the Org Based Communications behavior under Stakeholder Management, where the model calls it storytelling plus data, and it is how a PM turns a spreadsheet into a decision. My core claim: the story is the unit of persuasion, and data is what makes the story believable. Neither works alone.

I have watched both failure modes, repeatedly. Data without narrative is a wall of charts that lets everyone draw their own conclusion, usually the one they walked in with. Numbers do not interpret themselves. A dashboard is not an argument. Narrative without data is a nice anecdote that any skeptic deflates with "do we actually know that?" A story without evidence is just a strongly held opinion. The combination is what moves people: a clear narrative that says what is going on and what we should do , with data placed at exactly the points where a reasonable person would otherwise doubt you. [!quote] The reframe I use People do not remember your numbers. They remember your story , and they trust it because of your numbers. Build the story first; recruit the data to defend it.

Most persuasive product narratives follow the same skeleton, the change arc every good story has: 1. The world as it is , the status quo, the metric today, the user's current pain. 2. The tension , what is wrong, what it is costing us, why it cannot continue. (This is where the problem data lives.) 3. The turn , the insight or opportunity. What we now understand that we did not. 4. The resolution , what we should do, and the outcome we expect if we do it. Then I sequence the data to serve each beat: the retention drop is the tension , the cohort cut that reveals why is the turn , the projected lift is the resolution . The numbers are not a separate appendix of evidence. They are load bearing beats in the story itself.

Storytelling can slide into spin, picking the chart that flatters the conclusion you already wanted. I hold a hard line here: the story has to be the honest read of the data, including the parts that complicate it. This connects straight to Fluency with Data: causation versus correlation, valid samples, statistical significance. A narrative built on a misread number is not persuasion. It is a time bomb that detonates the moment someone checks your work. Credibility is the asset, and one caught exaggeration spends years of it. [!tip] Find the one number Every strong data story has a single hero metric, the one figure that, once the audience grasps it, makes the conclusion feel inevitable. Before I build the narrative I ask: what is the one number that changes the room? Everything else supports it. A deck with twenty equally weighted charts has no hero, and so it has no point.

The story bends to the audience, same as all Communication: Executives get the hero number and the recommendation first (Executive Communication), the "so what," not the cohort analysis. Your team gets the full arc, because they are going to build the resolution, so they need the reasoning. Skeptical stakeholders get the most data, placed precisely at their objections.

AI is genuinely good at the assembly: pulling numbers, drafting the chart, even proposing a narrative arc from a dataset (How AI Is Reshaping Product Management). That removes real grunt work, but it sharpens the human contribution rather than removing it. The persuasive frame, which tension matters, which number is the hero, what the honest story actually is, is judgment AI does not have. And in an org where everyone can generate ten charts in a minute, the burden of choice goes up : the scarce skill becomes deciding which single story is worth the room's attention. AI makes charts cheap. It makes a true, well chosen story more valuable.

This is the central operating reality of being a PM, and the thing that makes the role genuinely hard. You are held accountable for outcomes you cannot command into existence. You "lead" a team that does not report to you, "own" a product you cannot direct by fiat, and "drive" a roadmap built by people who can push back, and sometimes should. The entire Influencing People area exists because of this gap.

sections.h: My point of view: influence over control, always
sections.t: The instinct, when you are accountable but powerless, is to reach for borrowed authority: "the VP wants this," escalating every disagreement, pulling rank you do not have. It works once . Then engineering quietly routes around you, and you have spent trust you will never get back. Real PM leadership is the opposite move. You get people to want to do the work, by being the person whose judgment they trust and whose reasoning they respect. Control is a withdrawal from a finite account. Influence is a renewable resource that grows with use. A PM who relies on control is always running out. A PM who builds influence compounds it. [!quote] The reframe Authority is given to you by an org chart. Influence is given to you by the people you work with, and it is the only kind of power a PM reliably has. Spend your career building the second kind, not borrowing the first.

The most repeated, and most damaging, metaphor in our field. I reject it, and so should you. A CEO has actual authority: hire, fire, set budgets, direct people who must comply. A PM has none of that. Calling the PM a "mini CEO" sets a trap from day one. New PMs start acting like they have command authority, issuing directives instead of building alignment, and the team revolts. The metaphor describes a power the role does not contain. [!warning] Why the myth is worse than just wrong It is not merely inaccurate. It is actively harmful , because it teaches the exact wrong behavior. The PM's real job is influence, persuasion, and service to the team, and the CEO frame teaches command instead. I dismantle this fully in What Is Product Management and the Myths and Misconceptions reference. The closest honest version: a PM is accountable like a CEO but has none of the authority , which is precisely why influence is the entire skill set.

If not authority, then what? Five sources of real PM power, all earned: Source How you build it Clarity Be the person who makes the confusing situation make sense, the clearest Communication\ in the room Credibility A track record of good calls and surprise free delivery (Outcomes Over Outputs) The "why" People follow reasoning, not orders. Always explain the trade off behind the decision Trust Banked through Cross Functional Collaboration long before you need to spend it The numbers A defensible argument, told well (Storytelling with Data) Notice none of these come from your title. They come from how you show up. This is also why character, not just competence, underwrites PM leadership (Character, Competency, and Craft). People grant influence to those they trust, and trust is a character judgment.

The mature endpoint of leading without authority. You will not win every argument, and you should not. When the decision goes against you and it has been made fairly (clear RACI and Decision Rights), the move is to commit fully and execute as if it were your idea. Sulking or quietly undermining torches the trust your future influence depends on. Disagreeing well and committing fully is, paradoxically, one of the most influence building things a PM can do. (The phrase disagree and commit traces to Intel's Andy Grove and was later popularized as an Amazon leadership principle.)

AI changes almost nothing here, and that is the point. As mechanical PM work gets automated, the durable core, what Lenny calls the people and glue, the influence and trust that no model possesses, becomes a larger fraction of the job (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates). AI can hand you a sharper argument or a cleaner deck, which is leverage. What it cannot do is earn an engineer's trust, hold a room, or make anyone want to follow. Leading without authority was always the irreducibly human part of the job, and the AI era only underlines it.

If Leading Without Authority is the philosophy, this is the daily practice. A PM sits at the center of a team they do not manage, across engineering, design, data, and GTM, and the product only happens when those functions move in sync. Brandon Chu's metaphor fits perfectly: the PM is "the API" every function calls to coordinate. This note is about being a good API: reliable, well documented, low latency, and trusted.

sections.h: My point of view: you are a connective tissue, not a boss
sections.t: The PM does not sit above the functions. They sit between them. This is the The Product, Design, and Engineering Venn made operational: your job lives in the overlaps, and your value is making the whole greater than the parts. Get this right and the team feels like a single organism. Get it wrong and you are a bottleneck everyone learns to route around. The mindset shift that matters is that you are in service to the team's effectiveness, not its commander. The best collaboration I have seen comes from PMs who ask "what do you need from me to do your best work?" more than they say "here is what I need you to build."

sections.h: The functions, and what each needs
sections.t: Each function has a different contract with the PM. Treating them identically is a rookie error. Function What they own What they need from the PM Engineering Feasibility, how it's built A clear problem, stable scope, the honest why behind trade offs; respect for their estimates Design Usability, the experience The user problem (not your solution), early involvement, room to explore (Design Sense and Critique) Data Truth, measurement Sharp questions, not "pull me some numbers"; involvement before launch, not after GTM / Marketing / Sales Commercialization, the market Enough lead time, a narrative to sell, honest dates (Internal and External Stakeholders) This maps to Cagan's ownership split across The Four Big Product Risks: the PM owns value and viability, design owns usability, engineering owns feasibility. Knowing what you do not own is half of collaborating well. The fastest way to lose a designer is to art direct, and the fastest way to lose an engineer is to dictate architecture.

Everything above runs on trust, and trust has unglamorous mechanics: Reliability. You do what you said, when you said. Boring, and the entire foundation. Credit and blame. Push credit to the team, absorb blame yourself. This single habit builds more trust than anything else I know. The honest why. Never "because the VP said so." Explain the real reasoning (Leading Without Authority). Defending the team. Take the heat from stakeholders so your team can focus. Air cover earns loyalty. This is why I treat collaboration as a character issue as much as a skill one (Character, Competency, and Craft). You can teach someone meeting hygiene. You cannot easily teach the generosity that makes a team trust them. [!warning] The feature team trap In a Product Teams vs Feature Teams, the PM is reduced to a backlog administrator handing pre decided work to engineering. Collaboration collapses into ticket passing, and the team becomes mercenaries. Real cross functional collaboration requires an empowered team solving a problem together, not a relay race of handoffs. If your "collaboration" is just routing specs to builders, the org structure is the bug.

The tightest version of this is what Teresa Torres calls the product trio : PM, design, and a tech lead, discovering and deciding together , continuously. I am a believer. When those three share context and make calls jointly (Continuous Discovery), you do not need heavy alignment ceremonies, because the alignment is constant and ambient. The trio is cross functional collaboration compressed to its highest bandwidth form.

AI removes coordination overhead : auto generated standup summaries, status syncing, meeting notes, draft handoff docs (How AI Is Reshaping Product Management). That is real, and it means less time spent relaying information between functions. There is a deeper shift too. As PMs and designers prototype directly with AI, some handoffs collapse entirely, and the trio gets even tighter. What a model does not supply is the trust, the read on what a teammate needs, or the generosity of pushing credit outward. AI lubricates the mechanics of collaboration. It does not supply the relationship.

This is the gateway skill into Team Leadership, specifically its Coaching & Performance behavior, and the one most people are quietly terrible at in both directions. We are bad at giving feedback because it is uncomfortable, and bad at receiving it because it stings. Getting good at both is, in my experience, the single highest leverage thing you can do for the people around you, whether or not you ever manage anyone.

sections.h: My point of view: feedback is a gift you owe people
sections.t: The kindest thing you can do for a colleague is tell them the truth about their work, clearly and early. Withholding feedback to spare someone's feelings is not kindness. It is a quiet betrayal that lets them keep failing in ways they cannot see. Niceness that costs someone their growth is not nice. This is also why coaching is the heart of leadership, not a side duty. Cagan calls it a manager's 1 responsibility , worth roughly half the job. The shift from "I do the work" to "I make the people who do the work better" is the transition into management (The Manager as Coach).

A few rules I hold to, several of them straight from the appraisal do's and don'ts in my leveling system: First hand and specific. On things you observed , anchored to examples, never rumor or "people are saying." Vague ("be more strategic") is useless. Specific ("in that review you led with the solution before the problem") is actionable. Job related, never personal. Critique the work, not the personality. "This doc buried the recommendation" is fair. "You're disorganized" is an attack. Both kinds, regularly, through regular touchpoints , not saved for the annual review. Feedback six months late is an ambush, not coaching. Do not sugarcoat, and do not only criticize. Both are failure modes in my model. Softening the message until it disappears is as bad as a barrage of only negatives. [!tip] Tailor to the person The Coaching & Performance behavior is explicit that feedback is tailored to each person's strengths and goals. A confident senior PM needs bluntness. A shaky APM needs scaffolding. Same content, different delivery. Coaching is not one size fits all, and the manager who delivers identical feedback to everyone is really just talking to themselves.

The half people skip, and the one that gates your own growth. If you get defensive, people stop telling you the truth, and you go blind. Assume good intent and just listen. Your first job is to understand it, not rebut it. Argue in the moment and you have taught them not to bother next time. Separate the signal from the delivery. Even clumsily delivered feedback usually contains a real signal. Do not discard the message because you did not like the wrapping. Close the loop. Thank them, and actually act on it visibly. Nothing makes people invest in your growth like seeing their input change your behavior. [!warning] Myth: feedback is a manager to report thing Feedback flows in every direction: to peers, to your own manager, up the chain. Some of the most valuable I have given and gotten was lateral. And you do not need a title to coach. A Later Product Manager\ mentoring an Associate Product Manager\ is doing exactly this work, which is why the skill matters long before Team Leadership formally "applies" to you.

AI can scaffold the mechanics : draft a review from your notes, summarize a quarter of 1:1s, suggest framing for a tough message (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates). It is genuinely useful for getting past the blank page on an uncomfortable write up. The substance, though, is irreducibly human: knowing what a person needs to hear, reading whether they are ready to hear it, and having the relationship that makes hard feedback land as care rather than attack. A model can wordsmith the message. It cannot do the caring, the noticing, or the trust. Coaching is the part of the job AI touches least.

title: Hiring and Building a Product Team

sections.t: This is the craft behind the Resource Management & Hiring behavior in Team Leadership, and the highest leverage decision a product leader makes. Every other leadership skill, whether coaching, alignment, or strategy, is a multiplier on the people you hired. Hire badly and you will spend years coaching around a problem you created in an afternoon. You cannot out manage a bad hire.

sections.h: My point of view: hire for character first, competence second
sections.t: The most important thing I have learned about hiring is that competence can be coached; character usually cannot. A skilled PM who is arrogant or a poor teammate does more damage than an unpolished one who is humble and trustworthy, and you can teach the second person the craft. Cagan's bar is competent people with character , and the order is not accidental. This maps directly onto how I think about people in Character, Competency, and Craft: Layer Coachable? Weight it in hiring Character (humility, integrity, empathy, resilience) Very hard Screen hardest here; it's your floor, not your stretch Competency (judgment, communication, leadership) Yes, over time Look for trajectory and slope, not just current state Craft (tools, techniques, frameworks) Easily Weight least ; the most teachable, least predictive layer Most interview processes invert this. They grade hardest on the most coachable layer (craft and tool knowledge) and barely probe the least coachable one (character). I run it the other way.

The first move in the behavior is attracting diverse, high potential candidates , which means hiring starts long before the interview. A strong Product Teams vs Feature Teams reputation, a clear pitch for why working here grows their career, and a genuinely wide net all happen upstream. If your pipeline is narrow, no interviewing rigor saves you. You are just choosing carefully among the wrong sample. Note the words: diverse and high potential , not "people like the ones we already have."

The behavior calls for holistic, evidence based, inclusive evaluation. In practice: Probe for evidence, not assertions. "Tell me about a launch" is weak. "Walk me through the hardest trade off you made and why" surfaces real judgment. Hunt for Product Sense and Judgment, not memorized frameworks. Structure it and calibrate. Same core questions across candidates, and a debrief that surfaces evidence over vibes. Unstructured interviews mostly measure "is this person like me," which is bias in a lab coat. Test the actual job. A working session beats a quiz. Watch them frame a problem and communicate a recommendation.

sections.h: Team shape: build a spiky team, not a roster of clones
sections.t: The most overlooked idea in team building. Per my The PM Competency Model, individual PMs are spiky , exceptional at a few competencies and average at the rest, and that is correct . Great products come from spiky people on a well rounded team . So the unit of "well rounded" is the team , not the person. [!tip] Hire to your gaps Before any hire, I map the team's collective shape against The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape. If the team is deep on execution and thin on data, the next hire is a data spiky PM, even if a second execution strong candidate "interviews better." Hiring more of what you already have feels safe and quietly makes the team weaker. You are assembling a complementary set, not collecting high scorers.

The last part of the behavior, align org structure to needs , is the leader's version of prioritization. As a team grows, how you split product areas (by Product Teams vs Feature Teams, by customer, by funnel stage) shapes what gets built. Org design is product design by other means, and at the Group Product Manager and Director and VP of Product levels it is a core part of the job.

AI is creeping into sourcing and screening: résumé parsing, candidate matching, first pass take home evaluation (How AI Is Reshaping Product Management). It is useful for volume but dangerous on bias, because a model trained on past hires will reproduce their skew, undermining the diverse and inclusive mandate the behavior requires. So I treat it as a logistics assistant, never a judgment substitute. The high stakes calls, reading character, sensing potential, deciding whether this person makes this team better, stay firmly human. There is a meta twist worth naming: as AI commoditizes the craft layer, hiring for the durable layers matters more, not less (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates). You are increasingly hiring for the things AI cannot do.

This is the map for a question every PM eventually brings me: what's next, and what does it actually take to get there? Most career ladder docs I've read are a list of titles with vague adjectives bolted on. "Senior PMs are more senior," which tells you nothing. The ladder in this Atlas rests on one strong claim, and the whole domain hangs off it. [!tip] The one idea that matters The same twelve competencies escalate in altitude , not in kind . A Principal PM and an APM are doing the same job: Feature Specification, Product Delivery, Stakeholder Management, all twelve from The PM Competency Model. What changes is the scope they apply it to, the autonomy they have, the ambiguity they absorb, and the influence they carry. You don't graduate out of the competencies. You operate them at a higher altitude. The four dials are detailed in What Changes at Each Level. That's why I won't treat a promotion as "learning new skills." Almost nobody fails at the next level because they lacked a new ability. They fail because they kept applying an old one at the wrong altitude. Think of a Senior PM still spec'ing single features instead of sequencing a roadmap, or a new GPM still doing the work instead of multiplying it through people.

After Senior Product Manager, the ladder forks. You stay an individual contributor and scale your influence without managing anyone, or you become a manager and scale your impact through other people . These are different jobs, and I want to be clear that they're peers, not a hierarchy. The senior IC track sits alongside the management track. It is not a consolation prize. The full side by side is in The IC and Manager Tracks, and how to actually choose is in The IC vs Manager Decision. Both tracks share IC track Manager track Associate Product Manager (shared) (shared) Product Manager (shared) (shared) Senior Product Manager (the fork) (shared) (shared) Staff and Principal Product Manager Group Product Manager (Distinguished PM) Director and VP of Product The Chief Product Officer

sections.h: The mastery spine underneath every rung
sections.t: Titles are the surface. Underneath them I track progress on a six stage progression of mastery , running from Gaining Experience to Recognized Thought Leader , and I apply it per competency rather than per person. Because you're spiky (see The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape), you'll sit at different stages on different competencies, and that's the point of the model, not a defect in you. The spine is the engine of the whole ladder: The Six Stages of Mastery. [!warning] The myth I fight most A title is not a level. I've met "Senior PMs" operating like APMs and APMs operating like Staff. The title is a label HR applies. The level is the altitude you actually operate at, measured against the competencies. When the two drift apart, you get a promotion problem or a performance problem. Leveling has to be evidence based, which is what Score Gated Leveling and Calibration and Promotion are for.

Read The Six Stages of Mastery and The IC and Manager Tracks first, because they're the framework everything else leans on. Then walk the rungs in order: Associate Product Manager to Product Manager to Senior Product Manager, then the fork into Staff and Principal Product Manager or Group Product Manager, then Director and VP of Product and The Chief Product Officer. What Changes at Each Level is the cross cutting summary, and The IC vs Manager Decision is the one you'll come back to when it's your turn at the fork. For the at a glance grids, use The Master Competency Matrix and Competency Quick Reference by Level.

Titles are the surface of The Product Career Ladder. This is what runs underneath. When I level or coach a PM, I don't ask "are they a Senior?" I ask, for each competency, what stage of mastery they're operating at. That's a far more honest question, and it's the engine the whole ladder hangs off. [!note] Lineage The competencies these stages grade come from the Reforge / Ravi Mehta "Peak Product Manager" model (see The PM Competency Model). The six stage mastery spine below, and the way I gate leveling on it, are my own layer on top.

Six stages, applied per competency , not per person: Stage What it looks like in practice 1. Gaining Experience Learning the competency by doing it, with support. Needs the problem framed for them. The Associate Product Manager\ zone. 2. Applies Practical Knowledge Does it independently on well defined work. The reliable Product Manager\. 3. Applies Advanced Knowledge Handles complex, ambiguous, interrelated work; finds the right level of detail without being told. The Senior Product Manager\ zone. 4. Subject Matter Expert The person others go to on this competency; coaches it; raises the bar around them. Staff and Principal Product Manager\ / Group Product Manager\. 5. Master of Discipline Defines how the discipline is practiced across the org: process, standards, methodology. Director and VP of Product\. 6. Recognized Thought Leader Recognized inside and outside the org as a definitional voice. The Chief Product Officer\, Distinguished PM. [!quote] The arc in one line You move from being taught the work , to doing the work , to mastering the work , to teaching the work , to defining how the work is done , to advancing the field itself. Knows → Does → Masters → Teaches → Defines → Advances.

sections.h: Why "per competency" is the whole point
sections.t: You do not sit at one stage, and this is the part I have to correct most often. Because PMs are spiky (The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape), a strong Senior PM might be a Subject Matter Expert in Product Delivery, Applies Advanced Knowledge in Strategic Impact, and still only Applies Practical Knowledge in Stakeholder Management. That isn't a problem to erase. It's a profile to manage, and the spine lets me describe a real human instead of forcing them onto a single number. [!note] How a "level" is really computed A level is a center of gravity across the twelve competencies, plus floor rules. You can't sit at stage 5 overall while a core competency languishes at stage 1. That floor logic is exactly how Score Gated Leveling gates promotions, and it's why Calibration and Promotion exists: to align where someone actually operates with the title they carry.

sections.h: The stages map cleanly onto every scale I use
sections.t: This spine isn't a separate system. It's the connective tissue between all the assessment tools in the Atlas, and the proficiency language lines up: This spine Proficiency / assessment scale Gaining Experience "Knows" / Needs Focus Applies Practical → Advanced Knowledge "Does" / On Track Subject Matter Expert "Masters" / Outperform Master of Discipline → Thought Leader "Teaches" / Leads See The Proficiency Scale for the scoring mechanics and The Competency Self Assessment for placing yourself. [!warning] Mastery is not tenure The most common error I see is treating years in seat as a proxy for stage. Tenure correlates with mastery; it does not cause it. I've worked with ten year PMs stuck at "Applies Practical Knowledge" because they did the same year ten times over. Stage is about the altitude of the work you reliably handle , not the date on your offer letter.

For the first three rungs of The Product Career Ladder, there's only one path. Everyone does the same job at rising altitude. Then, after Senior Product Manager, the ladder forks, and a lot of careers get confused right here. So let me lay the two tracks out plainly.

sections.h: The fork, and where it opens
sections.t: [!tip] The fork is after Senior PM, not before Up to and including Senior PM, you're a pure individual contributor and there's nothing to choose. The fork opens because a strong Senior PM has more value to give than one product area can absorb. You can scale that value two ways: wider influence as an IC , or leverage through a team as a manager. That's the decision in The IC vs Manager Decision.

sections.h: The two tracks, side by side
sections.t: IC track Manager track Rungs above Senior Staff and Principal Product Manager\ Group Product Manager\ → Director and VP of Product\ → The Chief Product Officer\ What you scale Depth and breadth of influence Output through other people Output is Your own work + the bar you raise Your team's work Core competency that lights up Strategic Impact, Product Vision and Roadmapping Team Leadership Headcount? No direct reports Direct (then indirect) reports Mastery stage (see The Six Stages of Mastery) SME → Master → Thought Leader, applied to the craft SME → Master → Thought Leader, applied to people and org

sections.h: They are peers, not a ranking
sections.t: This is the point I hammer hardest. A Staff PM and a GPM sit at the same level : same compensation band, same influence expectation, with a different shape of contribution. The senior IC track is not where you go when you "can't" manage. Some of the highest leverage product people I've worked with never managed a soul. They shaped strategy across an entire portfolio and made every team around them better. Treating the IC ceiling as lower than the manager ceiling is how companies lose their best builders. [!note] What flips at the fork On the IC track, the twelve competencies keep escalating but Team Leadership expresses as influence and coaching without authority (Leading Without Authority) rather than formal people management. On the manager track, Team Leadership becomes the dominant competency, and your evaluation increasingly rests on whether your team succeeds, not whether you ship. See The Manager as Coach. [!warning] Two myths to kill Myth 1: "Management is the promotion." It isn't. It's a lateral move into a different job, detailed in The IC vs Manager Decision. Myth 2: "You can't go back." You can, and I've watched great PMs flip both ways more than once. The tracks are a fork in the road, not a one way door.

sections.h: How leveling works across the fork
sections.t: Both tracks are scored against the same The PM Competency Model. What changes is the altitude and which competencies dominate. The full grid lives in The Master Competency Matrix, and the per rung cheat sheet is Competency Quick Reference by Level. Promotions on either track are gated the same way, through Score Gated Leveling and Calibration and Promotion.

The first rung of The Product Career Ladder, and the one I think about most carefully, because the whole point of this level is learning , and the most common mistake is leveling someone to it and then expecting independence they haven't earned yet. [!tip] My one line definition An APM is a PM who can execute on a defined problem with support . The job isn't to be right alone. It's to learn the craft fast by doing real work next to people who can catch the falls.

This is the "Gaining Experience" stage of The Six Stages of Mastery made concrete. In role architecture terms: Dial At APM Complexity Assists in market research, supports development, helps create product docs; collaborates to gather requirements. Autonomy General supervision; dependent on others; routine work; limited scope. Scope A feature or a slice of one, not a product area. Ambiguity Problems arrive pre framed by a manager. The APM solves; they don't yet define. That "dependent on others / general supervision" line isn't a knock. It's the design of the level. See how these dials move in What Changes at Each Level.

The headline for almost every competency is the same phrase: with manager support. An APM exercises the full The PM Competency Model, just at low altitude and with a safety net. Competency What good looks like at APM Feature Specification Writes a clear spec for a minor feature; contributes to major ones, with support. Product Delivery Coordinates a small, defined piece of delivery with the immediate team. Product Quality Catches and reports quality issues; escalates the judgment calls. Fluency with Data Pulls and reads data to answer a specific question; doesn't yet drive the question. Voice of the Customer Sits in on research; synthesizes findings with help. User Experience Design Gives basic Design Sense and Critique\; defers on the hard trade offs. Business Outcome Ownership Understands the goal of their feature and how it's measured. Strategic Impact Learning the company's business model; not yet shaping strategy. Product Vision and Roadmapping Contributes to the roadmap; doesn't own one. Stakeholder Management Manages a few immediate stakeholders with coaching. Team Leadership Not applicable. No reports; not yet expected to influence the team. Managing Up Learning to give a clean status update and ask for help early. [!note] Team Leadership is genuinely "Not applicable" here I want to be explicit, because the Reforge/Hunt matrix this model derives from marks Team Leadership as Not applicable at APM (and at early PM). Leading a team isn't a "weak" APM skill. It's not part of the job yet . It begins informally only once someone is operating reliably as a Product Manager.

sections.h: How to be a great APM (and get promoted)
sections.t: Get the problem framed, then own the solution end to end. Your manager's job is to define what and why ; yours is to drive the how without dropping it. Reliability is the currency here. Use the safety net out loud. Ask early, ask specifically, show your reasoning. "Support" is a feature of the level, not an admission of failure. Master execution first. The fastest APM to PM jumps I've seen were built on rock solid Feature Specification and Product Delivery, the floor of Product Execution, and not on premature strategy takes. Watch your manager manage. This is your first, free look at the The IC and Manager Tracks. Pay attention to what coaching actually is (The Manager as Coach). [!warning] The trap Trying to look senior. APMs who skip the learning to perform "strategic thinking" tend to have shaky fundamentals underneath. Nobody gets promoted out of APM for opinions. They get promoted for reliable, independent execution on increasingly complex work.

You move up when you can take a defined product problem and run it independently , without the safety net catching you. The tenure and floor mechanics are in Score Gated Leveling; the next rung is Product Manager.

The load bearing rung of The Product Career Ladder. If Associate Product Manager is the learning level, PM is the doing level, the first point where you own a product area and the safety net comes off. Most of the people executing real product work in any company sit here, and getting good at this rung is what the entire craft is built to produce. [!tip] My one line definition A PM owns a product area independently and delivers outcomes on it. The defining shift from APM isn't skill, it's autonomy . You no longer need the problem handed to you fully framed; you can take an ambiguous ish area, do the discovery, and drive it to a result.

This rung spans what the role architecture splits into PM II and PM III: "Applies Practical Knowledge" maturing toward "Applies Advanced Knowledge" on The Six Stages of Mastery. Dial At PM Complexity Defines requirements, manages the product lifecycle, leads development projects; prioritizes from feedback and analyzes performance. Autonomy Limited supervision early; by PM III, general direction but sets own priorities . Begins to impact multiple projects. Scope A whole product area, owned end to end. Ambiguity Can operate on ill defined work; does the framing themselves rather than waiting for it.

The headline phrase flips from APM's " with support " to " independently ." A PM runs the full The PM Competency Model on their own area. Competency What good looks like at PM Feature Specification Specs major, complex functionality independently; finds the right level of detail; uses comps and prototypes. Product Delivery Owns delivery for the area; runs the The 10 50 90 Execution Framework\ with minimal surprises. Product Quality Makes Sound Product Decision Making\ across the relevant devices and use cases. Fluency with Data Drives the question, not just the answer; runs Experimentation and A B Testing\ and reads them honestly. Voice of the Customer Runs User Research and Discovery\ and translates it into the roadmap. User Experience Design Partners with design as a peer; owns the usability/feasibility trade offs for the area. Business Outcome Ownership Owns an Outcomes Over Outputs\, not a feature list; accountable for moving a metric. Strategic Impact Connects their area to company strategy; contributes upward. Product Vision and Roadmapping Owns and defends a roadmap for the area. Stakeholder Management Manages the full Internal and External Stakeholders\ set around the area independently. Team Leadership Begins to apply informally , mentoring an APM, influencing the team, even with no direct reports. Managing Up Managing Up\ proactively; brings problems with options, not just problems. [!note] Where Team Leadership wakes up At APM, Team Leadership is Not applicable . At PM it switches on informally : the late PM is the first level expected to influence the team and mentor more junior PMs, without yet owning anyone's career. This is the seed that, at the fork, grows into either Leading Without Authority\ (IC track) or formal management.

sections.h: How to be a great PM
sections.t: Own the outcome, not the output. The PMs who plateau here measure themselves by features shipped. The ones who get promoted measure themselves by the metric that moved. Internalize Outcomes Over Outputs. Get spiky on purpose. This is where your The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape starts to matter. Lean into your strong competencies; build a team around the rest. Run a tight execution machine. Reliable delivery (Product Delivery, Agile Delivery and Team Cadence) is the floor that buys you the right to be heard on strategy. Start influencing without authority. Practice the muscle now (Leading Without Authority); it's load bearing on both sides of the The IC and Manager Tracks ahead. [!warning] The plateau The most common career stall I see is the "permanent PM": reliable, well liked, shipping steadily, but never escalating scope or ambiguity . They do one good year, repeatedly. Promotion to Senior requires reaching for complexity you're not yet comfortable with , not polishing what you've mastered.

You move up when you reliably handle complex, interrelated, ambiguous work and your influence visibly extends beyond your own area. That's the threshold where the The IC and Manager Tracks starts to come into view. Next rung: Senior Product Manager. Mechanics in Score Gated Leveling.

The pivot of The Product Career Ladder. Senior PM is the last rung everyone shares, and it's the rung where the IC versus manager fork opens. Almost every PM I've coached hits their hardest career decision at or just after this level, so I treat it as a destination in its own right, not a waypoint. [!tip] My one line definition A Senior PM applies the craft to complex, ambiguous, interrelated work and visibly raises the people around them. The shift from Product Manager isn't "owns an area," because they already did that. It's operating at a higher altitude of complexity while their influence spills past their own area.

"Applies Advanced Knowledge" on The Six Stages of Mastery, beginning to touch Subject Matter Expert. Dial At Senior PM Complexity Develops and executes strategies (not just roadmaps); drives innovation; provides technical leadership; aligns with executives. Autonomy Functions independently; a role model for the team. Scope A complex area, or a set of interrelated areas; influence extends across the team. Ambiguity Operates comfortably in ill defined space and brings others clarity in it.

sections.h: The twelve competencies at Senior PM
sections.t: Everything from Product Manager holds, now at higher altitude, and three competencies in particular step up. Competency What steps up at Senior Feature Specification Sequences multiple interrelated specs; weighs all devices/points of sale even when a launch targets a subset. Strategic Impact Moves from connecting to strategy toward shaping it; thinks in business terms (Business Acumen and Models). Product Vision and Roadmapping Owns a Crafting a Product Vision\ for a complex area and evangelizes it upward and across. Team Leadership Now an explicit expectation. Mentors PMs, models strong practice, raises the team's bar (still often without formal reports). (the other nine) All present, escalated in scope and autonomy. See The Master Competency Matrix. [!note] This is where the spike has to pay off By Senior, a flat profile stops working. The strongest Senior PMs I've leveled are deeply spiky (The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape), genuinely excellent at three or four competencies and trusted to lead with them. Polished mediocrity across all twelve reads as "good PM," not "Senior."

[!warning] The decision you can't avoid much longer Past Senior, you can't keep climbing as an unspecialized IC. The ladder forks: scale your influence as a senior IC (Staff and Principal Product Manager), or scale your impact through people as a manager (Group Product Manager). Both are real promotions; neither outranks the other. The two paths are laid out in The IC and Manager Tracks, and the how to choose is The IC vs Manager Decision. You do not have to decide today , but a strong Senior PM should know which way they're leaning and start building the relevant muscles. The tell I look for: when a Senior PM's value clearly exceeds what one product area can absorb, it's fork time. Do they want to spread that value wide and deep into the craft , or multiply it through a team ? There is no wrong answer here, only an honest one, so be honest with yourself about which kind of work you actually want to do all day.

sections.h: How to be a great Senior PM
sections.t: Trade up your scope, not just your output. Reach for the ambiguous, cross cutting problems nobody owns. That's where Senior level reputation is made. Lead before you're asked to. Mentor, set standards, unblock peers (Coaching and Feedback). You're auditioning for both forks at once. Get fluent upward. Executive Communication and Managing Up become non negotiable; you're now in the room with leaders, framing strategy in their terms. Be honest about the fork. Don't drift into management because it's "next." Read The IC vs Manager Decision before you let momentum decide for you.

To Staff and Principal Product Manager: company level influence and SME depth, without taking on people. To Group Product Manager: the appetite and aptitude to make a team's output your output. Floor and tenure mechanics: Score Gated Leveling and Calibration and Promotion.

The senior IC track of The Product Career Ladder, and the path that proves you can have company level impact without managing a single person. This is the rung most companies under invest in and most PMs misunderstand, so I'll be blunt about what it actually is. [!tip] My one line definition A Staff/Principal PM is a Senior PM whose influence has outgrown a single team, and who scales that influence through the craft and the org , not through headcount. Their leverage is multiplying quality and judgment across teams they don't manage. This is one half of the fork that opens after Senior Product Manager (the other is Group Product Manager). See The IC and Manager Tracks.

Subject Matter Expert maturing toward Master of Discipline and, at the very top (Distinguished PM), Recognized Thought Leader. See The Six Stages of Mastery. Rung Complexity Autonomy Staff PM Manages timelines/deliverables, ensures quality; builds key stakeholder relationships. Strong autonomy; mentors; defines context ; leads projects forward. (Senior Staff) Sets vision for PM initiatives ; drives methodology innovation; represents PM at the exec level. Independent leadership through teams (by influence, not management); mentors others. Principal PM Defines overall product strategy ; oversees product activities broadly; industry facing. Fully autonomous; recognized thought leader and strategic advisor; leads high value initiatives. The throughline: defines context for others and leads through influence. A Principal can move company strategy without a single direct report.

sections.h: The competencies at the senior IC level
sections.t: The same The PM Competency Model, escalated to org altitude, with the strategy and influence competencies dominant. Competency What it looks like here Strategic Impact Defines product strategy across multiple areas; a primary strategic advisor to leadership. Product Vision and Roadmapping Sets vision for whole initiatives; reconciles roadmaps across teams. Feature Specification / Product Quality Reviews and coaches the org's specs and quality bar; improves the process itself . Stakeholder Management Operates with the most senior internal and external stakeholders. Team Leadership Expressed as Leading Without Authority\ . Raises the whole function's bar, mentors widely, but owns no one's career. Managing Up A true peer to executives in framing direction (Executive Communication). [!note] How Team Leadership works without a team On the IC track, Team Leadership does not go away. It transforms. Instead of formal coaching of direct reports (The Manager as Coach), it's product wide influence : improving processes, tools, and standards; spreading learning; mentoring across teams. A Principal makes everyone better without anyone reporting to them. That's the entire value proposition of the rung.

sections.h: Why this track is a peer to management, not a runner up
sections.t: [!warning] The myth that wastes great builders "Staff is for people who can't manage." Wrong, and expensive. A Staff PM and a Group Product Manager are the same level : same band, same influence expectation. The best Principal PMs I've worked with chose not to manage so they could stay close to the craft and the strategy, and they were worth more there than they'd have been running a team. If your company's IC ceiling is lower than its manager ceiling, your org chart is telling your best builders to leave.

sections.h: How to be a great senior IC
sections.t: Pick problems no single team owns. Your mandate is the cross cutting, the ambiguous, the strategically important. If a Product Manager could do it, it's beneath this rung. Multiply, don't hoard. Your output is partly other people's improved output. Coach widely (Coaching and Feedback); set standards; write the playbooks. Earn influence without a title to lean on. This is Leading Without Authority as a full time job. Credibility, Product Sense and Judgment, and Storytelling with Data are your only levers. Go external (toward Principal/Distinguished). Thought leadership outside the org is part of the top of this track.

The first people management rung of The Product Career Ladder, and the rung where the job changes the most. GPM is the management track twin of Staff and Principal Product Manager: same level on the ladder, completely different shape. If you take only one idea from this note, take this one: at GPM, your output stops being your work and becomes your team's work. [!tip] My one line definition A GPM is a Senior PM who now scales through people .
