They typically still own a slice of product directly, and manage a small team of PMs, which makes this the hardest transition on the whole ladder, because it's the first time "doing it yourself" is the wrong answer. This is one half of the fork after Senior Product Manager; the other is Staff and Principal Product Manager. See The IC and Manager Tracks.

The first rung of the management mastery progression: Subject Matter Expert in the craft, Gaining Experience in management. See The Six Stages of Mastery. Dial At GPM Complexity Sets priorities; translates objectives into operational plans ; builds cohesive operating rhythms for the team. Autonomy General direction; manages the day to day for one function (a small PM team). Scope A group of related product areas, owned through the PMs who run them. Ambiguity Absorbs ambiguity on behalf of the team and converts it into clear plans they can run.

sections.h: The competency that takes over: Team Leadership
sections.t: At every prior rung, Team Leadership was Not applicable (Associate Product Manager), then informal (Product Manager / Senior Product Manager). At GPM it becomes the dominant competency and acquires formal weight. [!quote] What Team Leadership now means Coaching & Performance (regular, tailored, actionable feedback; model strong practice) · Team Impact (align the team's goals to the business; hold the team accountable for outcomes, not activity ; remove blockers) · the first taste of Resource Management & Hiring (define role expectations; help Hiring and Building a Product Team). The other eleven competencies don't vanish, but you increasingly exercise them through your PMs. You're no longer the one writing every Feature Specification; you're the one ensuring your team's specs are excellent (Coaching and Feedback). [!warning] The transition trap (the one that ends careers) Doing the work instead of multiplying it. New GPMs, especially great former ICs, swoop in and do their PMs' jobs because they can do it faster. It feels productive. It is quietly fatal , because it caps your output at one person, starves your PMs of growth, and earns you a reputation as a bottleneck. Your job is now to make them great. The hardest skill to learn here is letting work be done worse than you'd do it, so a person can grow. See The Manager as Coach.

sections.h: How to be a great GPM
sections.t: Reframe your scoreboard. Stop counting what you shipped. Start counting whether your team hit its outcomes and whether each PM grew. That's the whole job (Outcomes Over Outputs). Coaching is the role, not a chore. The literature is right that coaching is the 1 manager responsibility, so plan to spend roughly half your time on it. The Manager as Coach, Coaching and Feedback. Hire ahead of need and level honestly. You now own Hiring and Building a Product Team and a seat at Calibration and Promotion. Bad hires and inflated levels are your debts to pay. Manage up and down at once. You translate exec strategy down into plans and your team's reality up into the room. Managing Up, Executive Communication. [!note] You can change your mind If you reach GPM, hate managing, and miss the craft, go back to the IC track. That's not failure; it's information. Plenty of strong product people round trip the The IC and Manager Tracks. Better an honest IC than a resentful manager. (The IC vs Manager Decision.)

You move toward Director and VP of Product when you reliably run a team to outcomes, develop PMs who get promoted, and start influencing function level decisions, managing through other managers , not just ICs. Mechanics: Score Gated Leveling.

The product leadership rungs of The Product Career Ladder, above Group Product Manager. The defining shift here is altitude of both people and strategy : you stop managing ICs and start leading through other managers , and you move from implementing strategy to setting it. This is where the job becomes mostly about organizational design and direction, and barely at all about shipping features yourself. [!tip] My one line definition A Director runs the product function for a domain, through managers, and translates company strategy into a coherent product strategy. A VP owns product strategy for a large slice (or all) of the business and leads through a management team . Both succeed or fail on whether the organization produces outcomes, not whether any one team does.

Master of Discipline on The Six Stages of Mastery, the level that defines how the discipline is practiced across the org. Rung Complexity Autonomy Director Implements strategy; leads complex issues to focused solutions; runs collaborative cross functional process across a department. Consultative direction across multiple teams; directs multilayered managers. (Senior Director) Moves from implementing to setting functional strategy; impacts org objectives and long term performance. Sets strategic direction across functions; begins to impact orgs outside their own; manages multilayered directors. VP Executes functional business plans; contributes to functional and business strategy ; collaborates with exec leadership. Full autonomy; leads through a management team . The throughline: leading through managers and setting (not just executing) strategy. A VP's hands are nowhere near a Jira board.

sections.h: The competencies at the leadership level
sections.t: The same The PM Competency Model, at the highest org altitude. Two clusters dominate. Competency What it looks like here Strategic Impact Sets functional/business strategy; anticipates industry risk and leverage points (Business Acumen and Models, The DHM Model). Product Vision and Roadmapping Owns the vision for a large portfolio; reconciles and funds roadmaps across many teams. Business Outcome Ownership Accountable for business line outcomes, not feature outcomes. Team Leadership Product wide leadership: builds the org structure, the Hiring and Building a Product Team\ machine, the standards, the product culture ; develops managers, not just PMs. Stakeholder Management Peer to other functional VPs; manages the most senior Internal and External Stakeholders\. Managing Up / Executive Communication Operates as an executive; frames product in P&L terms to the CEO and board. [!note] The work is now organizational design At this altitude, "doing product" means deciding how product gets done : team topology (Product Teams vs Feature Teams), the operating model (The Product Organization Matrix), how strategy cascades (OKRs and Goal Setting), and who you hire and promote (Calibration and Promotion). You're building the system that builds the product.

sections.h: How to be a great product leader
sections.t: Set context, don't give answers. The EMPOWERED team model is right: your job is to provide strategic context (vision, strategy, principles, OKRs and Goal Setting) and then let empowered teams solve problems. Hand teams problems to own, not solutions to build. Build the bench. Your legacy is the managers and PMs you develop and the Hiring and Building a Product Team, not your own roadmap. Promote your replacement. Defend product team culture. Hold the line against the Product Teams vs Feature Teams drift that pressure and scale invite. Culture is set by what leaders tolerate . Communicate as an executive. Executive Communication and Storytelling with Data are now core craft; you sell strategy to peers and the board, not features to a team. [!warning] The myth at this altitude "A VP is just a really senior PM." No. The competencies are the same, but the job is organizational leadership. Most of your day is people, strategy, and politics, and almost none of it is product execution. PMs who get promoted on execution and then keep executing make terrible Directors, because the thing that earned the title is not the thing the title now asks for. (What Changes at Each Level.)
The top product rung is The Chief Product Officer: product in the C suite, owning the function company wide and co owning company strategy.

The top of The Product Career Ladder, and product's seat in the C suite. By the time someone reaches CPO, the question is no longer "are they a good PM?" The twelve competencies are assumed mastered; the job is now company leadership through the product lens. This is the rung where The Six Stages of Mastery is the baseline, not the stretch. [!tip] My one line definition The CPO owns the product function company wide and co owns company strategy as a peer to the CEO, CTO, and CFO. They are accountable for whether product (as a discipline, an org, and a strategy) drives the business. Their "team" is every product person in the company, led through a layer of VPs.

A CPO sits at the intersection the whole craft is built on (customer, business, technology), but at company scale. The job has three faces: Face What it means Strategy Co author of company strategy; decides where the company plays and how product wins (The DHM Model, Business Acumen and Models). Organization Owns the product operating model, the leveling/career system, and product culture across the whole org. The executive team A peer to the CEO/CTO/CFO; the voice of the customer and the product in every company level decision.

All The PM Competency Model are table stakes. What separates a CPO is the altitude , and three competencies are now nearly the entire job: Competency At CPO Strategic Impact Sets company strategy through product; this is the dominant competency. Team Leadership Leads the whole function through VPs; sets product culture, the leveling system, the hiring bar; and is judged on the health of the org , not any product. Managing Up / Executive Communication "Up" is now the board . Frames product strategy in company financial terms to investors and the CEO. Stakeholder Management Aligns the entire executive team and external partners around the product strategy. Product Vision and Roadmapping Owns and evangelizes the company product vision (Crafting a Product Vision). [!note] EMPOWERED, at company scale Cagan's product leadership model is the right lens for this rung: leadership is strategic context + coaching + staffing. A CPO provides the context (vision, strategy, principles) that lets empowered Product Teams vs Feature Teams across the company make good decisions, and builds the org and culture that produces strong product people. They don't make product decisions; they build the system that makes good product decisions reliably.

Lead through context, not control. A CPO who's still making feature calls has failed to scale. Set strategy and principles; let the org execute (OKRs and Goal Setting). Own the product culture deliberately. Whether the company runs empowered product teams or a Product Teams vs Feature Teams is, ultimately, the CPO's choice, expressed through what they fund, reward, and tolerate. Build and defend the leveling system. The entire ladder in this domain is, at scale, a CPO's responsibility: that levels mean something, that Calibration and Promotion is honest, that Score Gated Leveling isn't title inflation. Be the customer's credible voice in the C suite. With Storytelling with Data and Executive Communication, translate user reality into terms a board will fund. [!warning] Two myths about the top "CPO = best PM in the company." No. This is a general management job with a product mandate. The best individual PM is often happier and more valuable on the Staff and Principal Product Manager. "It's the only summit." Also no. A Distinguished PM (the IC apex) and a CPO are both ends of the ladder (The IC and Manager Tracks): one leads the company , the other advances the craft . Two different summits, not a higher one and a lower one.

If the competencies don't change as you climb The Product Career Ladder, and I argue in the The Product Career Ladder that they don't, then something has to. This note names that something. There are exactly four dials, and every promotion is a turn of some combination of them. [!tip] The four escalation axes Scope · Autonomy · Ambiguity · Influence. Master these and you can read any ladder, at any company, regardless of how the titles are spelled. They're the machinery under The Six Stages of Mastery.

Axis The question it answers Low → High Scope How big is what you own? One feature → a product area → a portfolio → a business → the company Autonomy How much do you decide alone? Execute a defined task → set your own plan → set the team's strategy → set company direction Ambiguity How undefined is the problem when it reaches you? Problem fully framed → ill defined → you define the problem Influence How far does your impact reach? Your own work → your team → the org → the market/field

Level Scope Autonomy Ambiguity Influence Associate Product Manager\ A feature Works under supervision Problem handed over framed Self Product Manager\ A product area Sets own priorities Frames the work themselves Begins to reach the team Senior Product Manager\ Complex / interrelated areas Fully independent; role model Brings clarity to ill defined work Across the team Staff and Principal Product Manager\ Cross team initiatives Defines context for others Tackles the most ambiguous problems Org wide (no headcount) Group Product Manager\ A group, through PMs Owns the team's plan Absorbs ambiguity for the team The team's output Director and VP of Product\ A domain / business line, through managers Sets functional strategy Defines which problems matter The function / org culture The Chief Product Officer\ The company Co sets company strategy Defines the problem space itself The company / the market

sections.h: The fork, read through the dials
sections.t: This is the cleanest way to see why The IC and Manager Tracks are peers, not a ranking: [!note] Same level, different dials maxed After Senior Product Manager, both tracks keep climbing, but on different axes . The IC track (Staff and Principal Product Manager) scales ambiguity and influence to their ceiling without adding headcount . A Principal's reach is enormous, yet they manage nobody. The manager track (Group Product Manager → up) scales scope and influence through people , by managing, then managing managers. Neither is "higher." They turn different dials. Choosing which is The IC vs Manager Decision.

sections.h: Why this is the most useful note in the domain
sections.t: Two payoffs I lean on constantly: It diagnoses promotion readiness. When someone wants the next level, I don't ask "are they good?" I ask which dial they need to turn and whether they've shown it. Most "ready for Senior" PMs are great at their current scope but haven't yet proven they can operate in higher ambiguity . That's the gap, named precisely. This is the substance behind Calibration and Promotion and Score Gated Leveling. It explains why people fail after promotion. Almost always, they turned one dial (usually scope) but not another (usually autonomy or ambiguity). The new GPM who still does the work didn't turn the influence through people dial. The new VP who still executes didn't turn autonomy/strategy . (Identifying Growth Areas.) [!warning] The dials don't move in lockstep A promotion is rarely all four dials at once. Senior→Staff cranks ambiguity and influence hard while scope barely moves. Senior→GPM cranks influence through people while the craft dials hold. Mistaking "more scope" for "promoted" is how companies over level builders into the wrong jobs.

The single hardest career question in The Product Career Ladder, and the one PMs most often get wrong, usually by not treating it as a decision at all, but as a default. After Senior Product Manager the ladder forks: stay a senior IC (Staff and Principal Product Manager) or become a manager (Group Product Manager). This note is how I coach people through that fork. [!warning] The myth that ruins this decision Management is not a promotion. It is a change of profession. Going from Senior PM to GPM is a lateral move into a different job, not a rung above the IC track (The IC and Manager Tracks are peers). Cagan puts it well: management is coaching plus staffing. If you take the manager fork to "get promoted," you'll end up doing a job you don't want, and doing it resentfully, which your team will feel. Pick the work , not the title.

sections.h: What each fork's job actually is
sections.t: Strip away the titles and look at what you'd do all day: IC track (Staff and Principal Product Manager\) Manager track (Group Product Manager\+) Your output Your own work + the bar you raise across teams Your team's work Your day Strategy, the hardest problems, the craft, cross team influence Coaching, Hiring and Building a Product Team\, 1:1s, unblocking, calibration The dial you max Ambiguity + influence (no headcount); see What Changes at Each Level Influence through people You're judged on The quality and reach of your thinking Whether your people and org succeed Core competency Strategic Impact, Leading Without Authority Team Leadership, The Manager as Coach

[!tip] Where does your satisfaction actually come from? Not where you think it should come from, but where it does. Two diagnostic questions cut through most of the fog: 1. When a PM on your team ships something great that you coached but didn't build, is that a genuine high, or a hollow one? If it's a real high, the manager track will feed you. If you feel a pang of "but I didn't make it," respect that signal. 2. Would you rather be the person with the deepest answer in the room, or the person who grew the people who have the answers? Neither is nobler. They're just different. Other things I tell people to weigh honestly: Management is mostly not product work. Roughly half a manager's time goes to coaching alone (The Manager as Coach), plus hiring, performance, and politics. If what you love is the craft , the discovery, the Product Sense and Judgment, the shipping, the IC track keeps you in it. The IC ceiling is real and high. A Principal/Distinguished PM shapes company strategy with zero reports. You are not trading away impact by staying IC. You're trading people leverage for craft and influence leverage . Aptitude isn't appetite. I've seen brilliant ICs who'd be fine managers but would hate it, and average ICs who light up the moment they're coaching. Capability is necessary; wanting it is decisive.

sections.h: How to de risk the choice
sections.t: Try management before you commit. Mentor formally, lead an intern, run a workstream with borrowed people. Practice Coaching and Feedback and Leading Without Authority and notice how it feels. A "tour of duty" into management is reversible. You can change your mind, both ways. Round tripping the fork is normal , not failure. Better an honest IC than a resentful manager (and vice versa). The tracks are a fork, not a one way door. Decide on data, not drift. Don't let the next open req or a flattering title make the call. Use the diagnostic questions, then choose deliberately. [!note] What this means for managers leveling their people If you manage PMs, do not push your best IC into management as the only reward for excellence. That's how you lose a great builder and gain a mediocre manager. Build a real senior IC track (Staff and Principal Product Manager) and reward both forks equally at Calibration and Promotion time. Talk about the fork before someone's at it, and see The Manager as Coach.

This is the domain where the rest of the Atlas stops being a reference and starts being useful. The PM Competency Model tells you what good looks like. This folder is about the harder question, the one I care about most: where are you against it, and how do you close the gap. For yourself, and if you manage PMs, for your team.

sections.h: My point of view: a competency model that doesn't drive growth is wall art
sections.t: I have built and run three different assessment instruments for real PM teams: a 12 competency matrix, a lighter 9 skill self assessment, and a score gated leveling system. Every one of them was worthless until it produced a conversation and a plan. A rubric that just ranks people is a performance management weapon. A rubric that tells someone exactly which two behaviors to grow next quarter is a gift. It is the same instrument either way. The difference is entirely in how you wield it. So this domain is built around a loop, not a scorecard. [!example] The assessment loop Assess (where am I?), then Identify (which gaps matter for my next level?), then Plan (3 areas, concrete tasks), then Coach (the manager's 1 job), then Calibrate & promote (was the bar actually met?), then reassess. Skip any step and the loop breaks.

I deliberately keep more than one tool, because different jobs need different precision. Instrument Scale When I reach for it The Master Competency Matrix (12 competencies) The Proficiency Scale\ Full annual leveling; the system of record The Competency Self Assessment (9 skill Eval Sandbox) The Proficiency Scale\ A lighter, faster self rating between cycles Score Gated Leveling (numeric bands) 1 5 per skill, summed When promotion needs a defensible, transparent floor [!warning] Myth: more measurement equals more fairness Three instruments is not three times the rigor. It is three times the chance to hide behind a number. I run one as the system of record per team and treat the others as lenses. Precision theater is the most common way assessment goes wrong.

Note What it covers The Competency Self Assessment Scoring yourself honestly against the 12 competencies (and the 9 skill lighter tool) The Proficiency Scale The two scales I use and what each level actually means Score Gated Leveling Numeric bands, floor rules that tighten with level, tenure gates, and my critique of gating Identifying Growth Areas Finding the two weakest competencies that matter for your next level Building a Growth Plan The structure: 3 growth areas, 3 small concrete tasks each, outcome based The Manager as Coach Cagan's claim that coaching is the bulk of a manager's job, and how I do it Giving and Receiving Feedback Direct, specific, first hand; the appraisal Do's and Don'ts Calibration and Promotion How promo decisions get made, calibrated, and defended Running a Performance Review The appraisal process and the prep self check Common Assessment Pitfalls Recency, halo/horns, grading to the median, activity vs impact

Assessment is the operational arm of The Product Career Ladder. The ladder defines the levels; this domain decides who is at which one. Coaching is the heart of Team Leadership and an extension of Coaching and Feedback. And the whole thing is downstream of progression itself, because leveling only makes sense against The Six Stages of Mastery.

Before anyone else rates you, rate yourself. I make every PM I work with do this, and I do it on myself once a quarter. The self score is usually not that accurate, and that is fine, because the value is somewhere else: the gap between your self score and your manager's is the single most useful coaching artifact I know.

sections.h: My point of view: the honest self assessment is the cheapest growth tool you have
sections.t: It costs an hour and a willingness to be uncomfortable. Most PMs skip it because the discomfort is real. Rating yourself forces you to admit, on paper, that you are a 1 on the thing you have been avoiding for two years. Do it anyway. The PM who can name their own weakness is already ahead of the one waiting to be told.

I use two instruments depending on how much precision the moment deserves. [!example] The full model: 12 competencies Rate yourself against all twelve competencies in The Master Competency Matrix, from Feature Specification through Managing Up, across the four areas of The PM Competency Model, on the The Proficiency Scale\. This is the system of record, so use it at review time. [!example] The lighter tool: the 9 skill Eval Sandbox When the full matrix is too heavy, I use a 9 skill self assessment adapted from the Almundo / Mind the Product talent model. Three categories, three skills each, scored The Proficiency Scale\: Holistic Comprehension : Product Thinking, UX, Business/Product Strategy Processes : Strategy Execution, Agile, Data Informed Management : Leadership, Communication, Proactivity Nine numbers on a radar takes fifteen minutes and is enough to spot your shape. It maps cleanly onto the four areas of The PM Competency Model without the ceremony. A third lens, Petra Wille's PMwheel (8 dimensions, 0 7), does the same job as a radar and is explicitly built as a self assessment and career conversation tool. Pick whichever one you will actually fill in.

The scoring is only as good as your honesty, so I enforce a few rules. Anchor every score to evidence. A "2 / Outperform" you cannot back with a specific example from the last quarter is a 1. If you cannot substantiate it, you do not have it yet; you aspire to it. This is the same standard I hold in Running a Performance Review. Score the behavior, not the title. "I'm a Senior PM so I must be On Track on strategy" is title reasoning. Score what you actually did, against the behaviors in the model. Expect to be spiky. Nobody is uniformly strong, and a flat profile usually means you are rounding everything to the middle. Real profiles have peaks and valleys (The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape). Spiky is normal. Flat is a smell. Rate against your current level first, then your next one. You are often On Track for today and Needs Focus for tomorrow. Both numbers matter, and the second one is your growth target (Identifying Growth Areas). [!warning] The two ways self assessment lies Impostor PMs under rate everything. They discount real wins as luck and grade themselves into the floor. Overconfident PMs inflate the strategic competencies, the ones with the least objective evidence, because that is where the prestige is. Both distortions cluster predictably, so knowing which way you lean is half the correction.

sections.h: What to do with the result
sections.t: The self assessment is step one of the loop, not the deliverable. Take it into a conversation with your manager, compare it against their read, and let the disagreements drive the discussion. Then convert the lowest scores that matter into a plan (Building a Growth Plan). A self assessment that ends in a folder instead of a plan was a waste of a good hour.

A competency is meaningless without a scale to measure it on. "Good at strategy" is a vibe. "On Track on Strategic Impact, with a clear gap on strategy definition" is a coaching plan. This note is the Rosetta Stone for the whole domain: the two scales I use, what each level actually means, and how they line up.

sections.h: My point of view: the scale is where most assessment quietly fails
sections.t: People obsess over the competency list and then bolt on a sloppy scale, the "1 to 5, you know, use your judgment" kind. That is where calibration dies. If two managers do not mean the same thing by a "3," your numbers are noise dressed as data. I would rather have a crude four competency model with a razor sharp scale than a beautiful twelve competency one rated on vibes.

sections.h: Scale 1: the 0 2 matrix rubric (the system of record)
sections.t: The Master Competency Matrix uses a deliberately coarse three tier scale. Coarse is a feature, because it resists false precision and forces a real call. Score Label What it means 0 0.5 Needs Focus Below the bar for the level. A real, named gap to coach against, not a verdict on the person. 1 1.5 On Track Meeting the bar for the current level. The healthy default, where most scores live. 2+ Outperform Exceeding the level, consistently operating like the level above. This is your promotion signal. The genius of three tiers is that "On Track" is the honest center of gravity. A team where everyone is Outperforming everything either has a once in a decade roster or, far more likely, a Common Assessment Pitfalls.

sections.h: Scale 2: the 1 5 mastery scale (the lighter tools)
sections.t: The 9 skill The Competency Self Assessment and my Score Gated Leveling use a finer five point scale built on a progression of independence: Score Label The real test 1 Inadequate (Gap) Can't do it yet, even supervised. 2 Supervised ( Knows ) Does it correctly with oversight. 3 Unsupervised ( Does ) Does it reliably on their own. 4 Generates ( Masters ) Sets the standard; others learn from how they do it. 5 Leads ( Teaches ) Defines the practice and grows it in others. [!tip] Knows, Does, Masters, Teaches is the spine That four word ladder is the most portable idea in this domain. Can you do it watched, alone, better than others, or teach it? It maps almost one to one onto The Six Stages of Mastery, from Gaining Experience up to Recognized Thought Leader, and gives you a fast gut check on any skill in seconds.

The two scales measure the same reality at different resolutions. Here is the crosswalk I keep in my head: 0 2 matrix 1 5 mastery Mastery stage Needs Focus (0 0.5) 1 2 / Knows Gaining Experience On Track (1 1.5) 3 / Does Applies Practical Knowledge Outperform (2+) 4 5 / Masters to Teaches Advanced, then SME and beyond [!warning] Myth: a higher number is always the goal No. The target depends on the level. An Associate Product Manager\ who "Knows" a skill under supervision is exactly where they should be, and pushing them to "Teaches" is malpractice, not ambition. Per level targets (for example the Eval Sandbox totals APM 14 / PM 22 / SPM 30 / LPM 38 out of 45) exist precisely so "good" is defined for your level, not in the abstract. See Score Gated Leveling and What Changes at Each Level. [!note] Why I don't use a single universal scale The 0 2 rubric is right for a rich behavioral matrix where nuance lives in the behaviors, not the number. The 1 5 scale is right for a self assessment radar or a summed leveling score where you need range. Forcing one scale onto both jobs is how you get either false precision or false coarseness.

One of the leveling systems I built took a different bet from the behavioral matrix. Instead of holistic judgment, it gated every level on a number. You score across a fixed set of skills, sum it, and the total, plus a few floor rules and a tenure clock, decides which level you qualify for. I have strong, mixed feelings about this approach, and this note is both the mechanics and my honest verdict.

The system scores two skill groups on the The Proficiency Scale\ (here labeled Need to improve, Minimum, Good, Advanced, Master): Hard skills : strategic thinking & planning, delivering against goals/KPIs, team management & accountability, reporting & stakeholder management, user science & empathy, data analysis & experimentation, process implementation, requirement & documentation building, market/competitor grasp, innovation & community building, leadership fundamentals. Soft skills : teamwork & respect, communication, problem solving, efficiency & accuracy, attitude, initiative, time management. Sum it all into a single number, then apply three kinds of gate. [!example] The gating logic Level Score band Floor rule Tenure APM 43 54 none none PM 55 67 no category below 2 1 yr as APM SPM 68 80 no category below 3, no required soft skill below 3.5 2 yrs as PM LPM 81+ no required skill below 4 2 yrs as SPM Three forces stack. The band is the primary gate: raw total competence. The floor rules tighten as you climb, so at PM you just can't be failing a whole category, and by LPM you can't be merely "Good" at anything that matters. Tenure is additive on top of both, because even a high scorer waits when some judgment only comes from reps.

sections.h: Why the floors tighten, and why the tenure gate exists
sections.t: This is the part of the design I am proudest of. A pure sum lets a PM buy their way up by being spectacular at two things and absent on five. The rising floor closes that loophole. Seniority is not just more skill, it is the elimination of holes. A Senior PM with a glaring gap is a liability precisely because the org now relies on them where they are weak. The tenure clock guards against the other failure mode, the fast learner who scores high before they have seen enough go wrong to have real Product Sense and Judgment. You can't cram experience.

sections.h: My verdict: numeric gating is a great floor and a terrible ceiling
sections.t: [!tip] What numeric gating gets right Transparency. Everyone sees the bar. "Why didn't I get promoted" has a concrete, defensible answer instead of a shrug. That kills a huge class of fairness complaints. Calibration anchor. A summed score is harder to fudge than a vibe, which dampens Common Assessment Pitfalls. Self service growth. A PM can see exactly which skills gate their next level and aim at them (Identifying Growth Areas). [!warning] What numeric gating gets wrong It implies skills are additive and fungible. They aren't. A 4 in stakeholder management does not offset a 1 in delivery, and yet the score says it does. It invents precision. The gap between a 64 and a 66 is noise, but the band makes it a verdict. See The Proficiency Scale on false precision. It can become the goal. The moment people optimize the score instead of the craft, you have built a Outcomes Over Outputs, activity dressed up as growth. It struggles with spiky talent. The brilliant, lopsided PM the The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape says you want is exactly who the floor rules punish hardest. So I use the number as a floor and a conversation starter, never the final call. It is excellent for answering "is this person clearly not ready" and for forcing an honest, transparent bar. It is dangerous as the thing that actually grants a promotion, because that decision belongs to calibrated human judgment (Calibration and Promotion), with the score as one input. The behavioral The Master Competency Matrix and its The Proficiency Scale\ carry the nuance the number can't. [!note] Pairs with the ladder, not instead of it Score bands are a mechanism. They don't define what each level means. That is the job of The Product Career Ladder and What Changes at Each Level. The score tells you whether someone clears APM to PM; the ladder tells you what being a PM actually is.

You've scored yourself (The Competency Self Assessment). Now comes the move that separates a development plan from a wish list: choosing which gaps to actually work on. Not all weaknesses are worth your time, and the most common mistake I see is people grinding on the wrong ones.

sections.h: My point of view: pick the two weakest competencies that matter for your next level, not your two lowest scores
sections.t: Those are different things. Your lowest raw score might be in a competency that is irrelevant to where you are going. Chasing it is how earnest PMs spend a year improving and still don't get promoted. [!example] The filter I use A growth area has to clear both screens: 1. You're weak at it. Needs Focus on the The Proficiency Scale\, or a 1 2 on the 1 5. 2. It gates your next level. The bar genuinely rises here at the level above you (What Changes at Each Level). Weak and load bearing. A gap that fails screen 2 is a "nice to have," not a growth area.

sections.h: Why not just fix your lowest score?
sections.t: Because of shape. The whole premise of The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape is that great PMs are spiky, and great products come from spiky people on balanced teams. Some of your low scores are genuinely fine. They are the valleys that let you have peaks, and a teammate covers them. Sanding every valley flat doesn't make you a great PM. It makes you a uniformly average one. The art is telling a fine weakness from a fatal one. [!warning] The exception: never let execution be a valley There is one place I don't tolerate a gap regardless of level, and that is Product Execution. You can be spiky everywhere else, but a PM who can't reliably ship isn't spiky. They are not yet doing the job. If a Product Delivery or Feature Specification gap shows up, it jumps the queue. Everything else is a multiplier on a base of: can this person get good product out the door.

sections.h: How to find the load bearing gaps
sections.t: A few methods, in the order I trust them. Diff your self score against your manager's. The competencies where you rated yourself high and they rated you low are pure gold. They are blind spots, the gaps you can't see, which is what makes them the most dangerous. Start there. Read the level above you, behavior by behavior. Don't read your current level's column. Read the next one in The Master Competency Matrix. The behaviors that make you wince, the "I don't do that yet" ones, are your gates. Look at the floor rules. If you are in a Score Gated Leveling, the next level's floor rule names your gap for you: the category you can't drop below, or the soft skill that must hit a threshold. The system is telling you what to fix. Ask what blocked you last quarter. Reframe missed outcomes as competency gaps. A roadmap that kept slipping usually isn't bad luck. It is likely a Prioritization Frameworks or Stakeholder Management gap wearing a disguise. [!tip] Two, not five Cap it at two competencies, at most three when you get to the plan. A PM "working on" six things is working on nothing, and focus is a competency too. The Building a Growth Plan structure enforces this on purpose.

Identifying growth areas is not only about gaps. Your peaks are how you create disproportionate value, and at senior levels you are promoted on the strength of your spike far more than the absence of valleys. So I name the top strengths in the same pass, but I invest in them to turn "Masters" into "Teaches" (The Proficiency Scale), which is a leadership move, not a remediation one. Fix the fatal valley, and amplify the defining peak.

This is where assessment turns into action. You have found the two or three gaps that matter (Identifying Growth Areas), and now you build the plan that closes them. I use one structure. It is deliberately small, and it has survived every team I have run it on.

sections.h: The structure: 3 growth areas, 3 small tasks each
sections.t: [!example] The template I use Pick your 3 growth areas (the load bearing gaps). Under each, define 3 small, concrete tasks , for nine in total. Each task is something you can do and finish, with a way to tell it happened. Task 1 Task 2 Task 3 Growth Area 1 … … … Growth Area 2 … … … Growth Area 3 … … … Three by three is not arbitrary. Three areas is the most a person can hold attention on while doing their actual job. Three tasks each is enough to build a habit without becoming a second full time role. Nine items feels small, and small is exactly why it gets done, where the ambitious twenty item plan dies in week two.

sections.h: My rules for a task that actually works
sections.t: The structure is easy. The discipline is in the tasks. A bad task is a vague intention. A good one is concrete, bounded, and evidence producing. It's a behavior, not an outcome you don't control. "Get promoted" isn't a task. "Lead the spec review for the Q3 launch and get sign off in one pass" is, and it demonstrates the Feature Specification competency you are growing. It's small enough to finish in weeks, not quarters. If it's big, it's a project, not a task. Break it down until each piece is a single, schedulable action. It produces evidence. This is the rule I push hardest. Every task should leave a trace you can point to in a Running a Performance Review: a doc you wrote, a meeting you ran, a metric you moved. No evidence, no growth you can prove. It's tied to your real work, not extra work. The best growth tasks are reframings of things you were going to do anyway. You have to ship the feature regardless, so the task is shipping it in the way that builds the gap skill. [!tip] Make it outcome based, not activity based "Read three books on strategy" is activity, and activity is the Common Assessment Pitfalls. "Write and defend a one page strategy for my area, and have my manager poke holes in it" is outcome based, because it ends in a thing that either holds up or doesn't. Books are inputs. A defended artifact is evidence. Always prefer the artifact.

sections.h: Worked shape (a pattern, not a real plan)
sections.t: Say your gap is Strategic Impact. The three tasks might be: (1) write a one page narrative connecting your area to a company OKRs and Goal Setting; (2) present it to your skip level and capture their pushback; (3) revise the Building and Managing a Roadmap to reflect the strongest objection. Each one is small, each leaves a trace, and each is real work you would do anyway, now aimed at the gap.

A plan you write once and never revisit is theater. I review growth plans on the same cadence as 1:1s. It is light touch, a "where are we on the nine," not a re litigation. The manager's job here is The Manager as Coach: unblock, give Giving and Receiving Feedback on the evidence as it lands, and adjust tasks that turned out wrong. When all nine are done, you reassess (The Competency Self Assessment) and write the next plan. That loop, assess and plan and do and reassess, is what deliberate growth actually is. There is no shortcut around the reps. [!warning] The plan is the PM's, not the manager's If I write the plan for someone, they don't own it and it dies. My job is to coach them to write a good one and then hold them to it. Ownership is the difference between a development plan and a to do list someone resents.

If you take one thing from this domain, take this: coaching is the job. Not a part of the job, not the soft side of it, but the central work of managing PMs. Everything else, the planning and reviews and hiring, exists to serve it.

sections.h: The Cagan claim I build on
sections.t: Marty Cagan, in EMPOWERED , argues that coaching is a manager's number one responsibility, and that, done right, coaching and staffing together take up the majority of a first level manager's week (Cagan puts it near 80%). When I first read that I thought it was an exaggeration. Having run teams, I don't anymore. For anyone with junior reports, it really is the job. Cagan splits the manager role into two halves: management (coaching plus staffing) and leadership (strategic context, vision, strategy). The coaching half is the one new managers neglect, because it is invisible, slow, and doesn't show up on a roadmap. [!quote] Why it's 1 Cagan's logic is that your team's results are capped by the capability of the people on it. You can't out strategize a team you haven't grown. The highest leverage thing a manager does is not making better decisions, it is creating more people who make better decisions. (Marty Cagan, SVPG / EMPOWERED.) This is also the moment Team Leadership stops being abstract. Its first behavior, Coaching & Performance, is this note. And the transition from doing the work to making the people who do the work better is the entire jump into management (The IC vs Manager Decision).

Coaching is not feedback (that is Giving and Receiving Feedback), and it is not the annual review (that is Running a Performance Review). It is the continuous, individualized work of growing a specific person. The 1:1 is their meeting, not my status update. The fastest way to ruin coaching is to turn the 1:1 into a project check in. Status has other channels. This time is for them: their growth, their blockers, and the stuff they are avoiding. Tailor it to the person. The model is explicit that coaching is tailored to each person's strengths and goals (Coaching and Feedback). A confident senior PM needs me to be blunt and get out of the way. A shaky Associate Product Manager\ needs scaffolding and reps. Identical coaching for everyone means I am really just talking to myself. Coach to the gap, not the symptom. A slipping launch looks like a delivery problem. Underneath, it is often a Prioritization Frameworks or Stakeholder Management gap. I trace the symptom back to the competency (Identifying Growth Areas) and coach that. Model the practice. The Coaching & Performance behavior includes modeling strong practices yourself. You cannot coach a Feature Specification standard you don't visibly hold. Hypocrisy is the fastest way to lose a team's trust. Create a safe space to learn. People grow by trying things slightly beyond their reach and sometimes failing. If failure gets punished, they stop reaching, and coaching dies. My job is to make the stretch safe. [!tip] The assessment tools are coaching tools The The Master Competency Matrix and The Competency Self Assessment aren't just for review season. They are the map a coaching conversation needs. Petra Wille makes this explicit: the PMwheel is built as a coaching and career conversation tool, not a grading instrument. As she puts it, great product leaders make strong product people. Use the rubric to find the conversation, not to deliver a verdict. [!warning] Myth: coaching means having the answers The worst coaching I have seen is a senior PM telling juniors exactly what to do. That builds dependence, not capability. Real coaching is mostly questions. What are the risks here? What does the data say? What would you do if I were on vacation? Questions like those grow the person's own Product Sense and Judgment. If your team can't function without you, you have been delegating tasks, not coaching people.

It is not therapy, it is not friendship, and it is not lowering the bar to be liked. The kindest coaching holds a high standard and gives people the support to reach it. Niceness that costs someone their growth is not actually kind (Giving and Receiving Feedback).

Almost not at all. This is the competency AI touches least. AI can summarize a quarter of 1:1s or surface team health signals (Competencies AI Commoditizes vs Elevates), but the core of coaching does not commoditize: knowing what a person needs to hear, reading whether they are ready to hear it, and holding the relationship that makes a hard truth land as care. If anything, as the mechanical PM work gets automated, growing the humans who do the irreducible work becomes a larger share of what a leader is for.

Feedback is the atomic unit of coaching. Get it right and growth compounds. Get it wrong and you either crush people or, more commonly, never tell them anything real until it is too late. This note is the mechanics, including the appraisal Do's and Don'ts straight from the governance model in my leveling system.

sections.h: My point of view: feedback is a gift you owe people
sections.t: The kindest thing you can do for a colleague is tell them the truth about their work, clearly and early. Withholding it to spare their feelings is not kindness. It is a quiet betrayal that lets them keep failing in ways they can't see. The most common feedback failure is not being too harsh. It is saying nothing real for months and then ambushing someone at review time with a problem they could have fixed in a week.

I codified these as appraisal governance, but they apply to any feedback, any day. [!example] Appraisal Do's Focus on job related feedback. The work, the behaviors, the outcomes, never the person's character. Give both positive and constructive. People need to know what to keep doing as much as what to change. Use first hand evidence. Things you observed yourself, anchored to specific examples. Make criticism constructive. Tied to a path forward, not just a complaint. [!warning] Appraisal Don'ts No personal or personality comments. "This doc buried the recommendation" is fair. "You're disorganized" is an attack on who they are, and it is useless. Not only negative. A barrage of criticism with no acknowledgment is demoralizing and gets tuned out. No rumors or allegations. "People are saying…" is not feedback. It is gossip with a tie on. If you didn't see it, you can't coach it. Don't sugarcoat. Softening the message until it disappears is as much a failure as cruelty. The person leaves the room feeling fine and learns nothing. The two failure modes, only negative and sugarcoating, bracket the whole problem. Coaching lives in the narrow band between them: honest and kind, specific and forward looking.

First hand and specific. Vague feedback like "be more strategic" is worse than none, because it implies a problem without naming it. Specific feedback ("in that review you led with the solution before you'd framed the problem") is something a person can actually act on. Regular, not annual. Give it through frequent touchpoints, not saved up for the review. Feedback six months late is an ambush. Feedback the same week is coaching. The annual review should contain zero surprises (Running a Performance Review). Tailor the delivery. Same content, different wrapping per person (The Manager as Coach). Bluntness for the confident, scaffolding for the shaky.

This is the half everyone skips, and the one that gates your own growth. If you get defensive, people quietly stop telling you the truth, and you go blind. Assume good intent and just listen. Your first job is to understand it, not rebut it. Argue in the moment and you have taught them not to bother next time. Separate the signal from the delivery. Even clumsily delivered feedback usually contains a real signal. Don't throw out the message because you disliked the wrapping. Close the loop. Thank them and then visibly act on it. Nothing makes people invest in your growth like seeing their input change your behavior. [!tip] The prep self check Before delivering hard feedback (or going into a Running a Performance Review), run the self check from my governance model. Have I considered their strengths and weaknesses? Can I substantiate each point with a specific example? Have I anticipated how they will react? If you can't substantiate it, don't say it yet. Go find the evidence first. [!warning] Myth: feedback flows downhill from managers It flows in every direction: to peers, up to your own manager, across teams. Some of the most valuable feedback I have given and received was lateral. And you don't need a title to give it. A senior Product Manager\ coaching an Associate Product Manager\ is doing exactly this work, long before Team Leadership formally applies to them (Coaching and Feedback).

This is where assessment gets political, and where most orgs do it badly. A promotion is the highest stakes assessment decision you make. It is expensive to reverse, it shapes the culture, and it is watched by everyone. Doing it with rigor instead of vibes is non negotiable.

sections.h: My point of view: promote for the level you're observing, not the one you hope for
sections.t: The bar is "already operating at the next level," not "ready to try it" and not "deserves a reward for tenure." This is the most common promotion mistake I see: promoting someone into a level as a bet, then watching them struggle in a role they hadn't yet grown into. A promotion should recognize a transition that has already happened, not gamble on one. If you are squinting, the answer is "not yet." [!tip] The Outperform signal On the The Proficiency Scale\, the promotion signal is consistently scoring Outperform (2+) at the current level in the competencies that define the next one. In other words, they are already doing the next job, just without the title. One brilliant quarter isn't it. Consistently is the word that matters.

sections.h: What calibration is, and why it's the antidote
sections.t: Calibration is the meeting where managers compare ratings across their reports to enforce a shared bar. Left alone, every manager grades on their own private scale. The generous ones inflate, the demanding ones deflate, and a "PM" ends up meaning something different on every team. Calibration drags those private scales into alignment. [!example] How a calibration session runs Managers bring scored assessments (The Master Competency Matrix) with evidence, not adjectives. Promotion candidates are discussed against the level definition (What Changes at Each Level), not against each other's personalities. The room pressure tests each case by asking whether this person would clear the bar on a different team. If the case only holds under this one manager, it is not a promotion case. It is a relationship. Borderline cases get named honestly and deferred with a concrete gap, not waved through. The goal is not to ration promotions to a quota. That is Common Assessment Pitfalls, a real pitfall. The goal is that a level means the same thing everywhere.

A promotion case is an argument, and like any argument it needs evidence, not assertion. Show next level work already happening. Point to concrete artifacts: the strategy they wrote, the cross team initiative they led, the Outcomes Over Outputs they owned. "They're ready" is a claim. The artifact is proof. Map it to the level's behaviors. Walk the next level's column in The Master Competency Matrix and show evidence against each one. Gaps that remain are honest reasons to wait. Use the score as a floor, not the verdict. In a Score Gated Leveling, the band and floor rules tell you whether someone is eligible. They don't tell you whether to promote. Calibrated human judgment does that. The number screens; it doesn't decide. Pre empt the obvious objection. Every strong case has one weak spot. Name it before the room does, with a plan. This is the Giving and Receiving Feedback applied to promotion: can I substantiate it, and have I anticipated the pushback? [!note] The governance rhythm In my model, career journey conversations start as someone nears the top of their level, not at the moment of promotion. Expectations are transparent year round, the move happens at the yearly review when the bar is genuinely met, and increases are approved up the chain. A promotion should feel earned and expected, never like a surprise to the person or a fight in the room. If it is a surprise, the coaching failed somewhere upstream. [!warning] Myth: promotion is a reward for hard work It isn't. It is a recognition that someone is already doing a bigger job. Hard work that doesn't add up to next level scope earns recognition, a raise, a thank you, but not a level. Conflating effort with level is how you end up with people stuck in roles that don't fit, resentful that loyalty didn't convert to advancement. Reward effort honestly, and reserve levels for demonstrated scope.

The performance review gets a bad reputation, almost always because it is run badly: saved up all year, sprung as a surprise, and turned into a one way verdict. Done right, it is a checkpoint rather than an event, a structured moment to confirm what both of you already knew. Here is the process I run and the governance behind it.

sections.h: My point of view: a good review contains zero surprises
sections.t: If anything in the review is news to the person, the coaching failed, not the review. The review's job is to summarize and formalize a year of feedback that already happened (Giving and Receiving Feedback), tie it to the The Master Competency Matrix, and set the next plan. A review that delivers a shocking verdict is a manager outsourcing twelve months of avoided conversations into one painful hour. Don't be that manager.

[!example] How I run a review cycle 1. Self assessment first. The PM scores themselves (The Competency Self Assessment) before I share my read. The diff between our scores is the most productive part of the conversation. 2. Manager assessment, evidence backed. I score the same model, anchored to first hand examples from the whole period, not just the last month (Common Assessment Pitfalls is the enemy here). 3. Calibrate before delivering. Ratings go through Calibration and Promotion so the bar matches other managers' before the PM hears a number. 4. The conversation. Walk the competencies, reconcile the diffs, and name strengths and gaps with evidence. It is two way, so I am getting feedback too. 5. The forward plan. End on a Building a Growth Plan: 3 areas, concrete tasks. A review that ends in a rating but no plan is half finished. Note the order: self assessment, then manager read, then calibrate, then talk, then plan. The review is one beat in a continuous loop, not a standalone judgment day.

Before I walk into any review, I run the same self check from my appraisal governance. It's three questions, and it has saved me from more bad reviews than anything else. [!tip] The prep self check Have I considered strengths and weaknesses? Not just the gaps. A review that is all criticism is a governance Don't (Giving and Receiving Feedback). Can I substantiate every point with a specific example? If I can't name the instance, I don't raise it. First hand evidence only, no rumors, no "people are saying." Have I anticipated the problems and reactions? Where will this land hard? What will they push back on? Walking in prepared for the hard moment is the difference between a conversation and an argument.

sections.h: Hold the Do's and Don'ts in the room
sections.t: The whole review runs on the appraisal Do's and Don'ts (Giving and Receiving Feedback): job related not personal, both positive and constructive, first hand evidence, constructive criticism. And the Don'ts matter most here, because the review is high stakes and on the record. No personality comments, no only negative pile on, no rumors, and no sugarcoating the message until it dissolves. [!warning] Myth: the review is where feedback happens The review is where feedback gets summarized. The actual feedback happened, or should have, in dozens of small moments across the year (The Manager as Coach). Treating the annual review as your feedback delivery mechanism is the single most common way to do this badly. By the time you are writing the review, the work is mostly assembly. [!note] Self review and the IC Even without reports, you run a version of this on yourself. Score honestly, gather your own evidence, ask your manager and peers for input, and write your own forward plan. Owning your review instead of waiting to receive one is itself a senior behavior, and it makes the actual review a formality.

Every assessment instrument in this domain (the The Master Competency Matrix, the The Competency Self Assessment, the Score Gated Leveling) is only as good as the human running it. And humans are biased in predictable, well studied ways. This note is the field guide to the traps I have fallen into and watched others fall into, with the countermeasure for each.

sections.h: My point of view: name the bias before you score
sections.t: You don't beat these by being smart or well intentioned. Every bias here operates on people who believe they are being fair. You beat them with process: pre committed criteria, first hand evidence, and calibration. Goodwill is not a control. Structure is.

sections.h: The five that wreck PM assessment
sections.t: [!warning] 1. Recency bias You weight the last month and forget the other eleven. The PM who shipped a win in week 50 looks great, and the one who carried Q1 and coasted lately looks weak. Countermeasure: keep a running evidence log all year, and at review time deliberately pull examples from every quarter (Running a Performance Review). If all your examples are recent, you haven't done the work. [!warning] 2. Halo / horns effect One vivid trait colors everything. The charismatic presenter gets rated up on Fluency with Data they are actually weak at (halo), and the quiet PM who shipped flawlessly gets rated down across the board because they don't perform confidence (horns). Countermeasure: score each competency independently, against its own behaviors, with its own evidence. Force yourself to justify why a strong communicator is also strong at Product Quality. Usually you can't, and the halo dissolves. [!warning] 3. Grading to the org's median The pull to cluster everyone around "On Track" or "meets expectations" because the extremes feel risky. This destroys the instrument's whole purpose. If nobody is ever Needs Focus and nobody is ever Outperform, your scores carry zero information and your Calibration and Promotion signal is gone. Countermeasure: demand evidence for the middle too. "Why is this a solid On Track and not a Needs Focus?" is the question that breaks the herd toward the center. A flat distribution is a smell, not a sign of fairness. [!warning] 4. Confusing activity with impact The deadliest one for PMs specifically. You reward the person who shipped the most features, ran the most meetings, and wrote the most tickets, while overlooking the one who shipped less but moved the number that mattered. Countermeasure: score against Outcomes Over Outputs. The question is never "how busy were they." It is "what changed in the business because they were here." A Outcomes Over Outputs looks productive on every activity metric and accomplishes nothing. [!warning] 5. The self assessment distortions They cut both ways. Impostors under rate, discounting real wins as luck. Overconfident PMs inflate the strategic competencies, the ones with the least objective evidence and the most prestige. Countermeasure: anchor to evidence, and read the diff between self score and manager score as the signal, not either number alone (The Competency Self Assessment).

[!note] Quieter traps Similarity bias: rating people who work like you higher. This one is lethal for Hiring and Building a Product Team, because the The T Shaped PM and Knowing Your Shape who covers your valley is exactly the one you will under rate. Idiosyncratic rater effect: your ratings say as much about you as about them. It is the single strongest argument for Calibration and Promotion. False precision: treating a summed Score Gated Leveling of 64 versus 66 as a real difference. The number is coarser than it looks (The Proficiency Scale). Potential vs performance conflation: promoting on "they could be great" instead of "they are already operating at the next level." See Calibration and Promotion. [!tip] The meta countermeasure Every trap above is neutralized by the same three habits: first hand evidence (no rumor), independent per competency scoring (no halo), and calibration (no private scale). If you only remember one thing, make it this: substantiate every score with a specific example, or don't give it. That single discipline kills most of these on contact.

This is my opinionated index of every framework in the Atlas, the tools I actually reach for, grouped by the job they do. I keep them in one place because the most common framework mistake is not picking the "wrong" one. It is not knowing which job you are doing, so you grab whatever is fashionable and start filling in its boxes as if the boxes were the answer. [!warning] A framework is a tool, never a box to fill in Frameworks do not produce decisions. They produce structured arguments . They force your criteria into the open and make your reasoning auditable instead of political. The output of any of these is an input to judgment , not a substitute for it. The moment a team treats "we filled in the RICE sheet" or "we ran a SWOT" as having decided something, the tool has become theater. Use them to force the conversation, then bring taste. And pick a few you will use consistently, because framework hopping every quarter destroys the one real benefit, which is comparability over time.

Find the job to be done , then the tool. Many frameworks live in other domains (discovery tools in Customer Insight, strategy tools in Product Strategy), but they are all indexed here so this is the one map you need.

sections.h: Prioritize: choosing what to do, and how hard
sections.t: Framework One line job Home RICE and Scoring Models Rank comparable features by Reach × Impact × Confidence ÷ Effort Strategy The Impact Likelihood Matrix Fast portfolio triage: Home Runs vs Stuff You Shouldn't Be Doing here The Kano Model What kind of value: basic, performance, or delight here MoSCoW Prioritization Scope a release: Must / Should / Could / Won't here The Eisenhower Matrix Personal/team triage by urgency × importance here The LNO Framework How much effort a task deserves once it's chosen here The Initiative Pyramid Organize bets by risk and escalation level Strategy Prioritization Frameworks The meta note: matching method to decision altitude Strategy

Framework One line job Home Jobs To Be Done Frame demand around the job, not the demographic Customer Insight The Opportunity Solution Tree Map outcome → opportunities → solutions → experiments Customer Insight Build Measure Learn and the MVP Learn cheaply under uncertainty; the MVP is an experiment here Hypothesis Driven Development Turn beliefs into falsifiable bets with kill criteria here Experimentation and A B Testing The rigorous read on whether a hypothesis held Customer Insight

sections.h: Strategize: where to play and why you'll win
sections.t: Framework One line job Home The DHM Model Strategy should Delight, be Hard to copy, Margin enhancing Strategy The North Star Framework One value metric + the inputs that move it Strategy OKRs and Goal Setting Set and track outcome based goals Strategy SWOT and Porter's Five Forces Classic company/market lenses, and their limits for product here The Initiative Pyramid Risk tiered initiative planning and escalation Strategy

sections.h: Decide: making the call and shipping it
sections.t: Framework One line job Home The 10 50 90 Execution Framework My execution OS: phases × confidence × roles Execution RACI and Decision Rights Who actually gets to make each call Execution Sound Product Decision Making One way vs two way doors; bias to action Execution theScore Product Principles Pre made decision rules for the team here Writing Good Product Principles The how to for authoring your own here

sections.h: Focus: allocating yourself and assessing your craft
sections.t: Framework One line job Home The LNO Framework Match effort to leverage; stop gold plating overhead here The Three Levels of Product Work Impact / Execution / Optics, and why teams talk past each other here The Eisenhower Matrix Defend Quadrant 2 important not urgent time here The PM Wheel Self assess the shape of your skills here The PM Competency Model The rigorous, leveled competency map Introduction

I built The Impact Likelihood Matrix and theScore Product Principles, and The 10 50 90 Execution Framework is theScore's. The rest belong to the people who created them: The LNO Framework (LNO and the The Three Levels of Product Work), Petra Wille (The PM Wheel), Noriaki Kano (The Kano Model), Eric Ries (Build Measure Learn and the MVP), Dai Clegg (MoSCoW Prioritization), Eisenhower/Covey, Humphrey (SWOT) and Porter (Five Forces). I keep the credit for a practical reason and not just a polite one. Borrowed frameworks carry their authors' assumptions, and knowing the source is how you know the limits.

Most prioritization frameworks ask which feature to build. Kano asks a better question: what kind of value does this feature create, and how does that value decay over time? That second clause is the part people forget, and it is the whole point. I reach for Kano when a team is arguing about table stakes versus differentiators and talking past each other, usually because they are scoring features on a single axis that does not actually exist. Noriaki Kano developed the model in 1984 to break a lazy assumption: that satisfaction is linear, that more feature equals more happy. It is not. Kano plots two axes: how present a feature is (horizontal) against how satisfied the customer is (vertical). Different feature types trace different curves through that space, and once you see the curves you cannot un see them.

[!note] The three that matter Basic (must be): Expected. Their presence earns nothing; their absence is a dealbreaker. A login that works, a checkout that does not lose your cart. You cannot win on these, you can only lose, so invest exactly enough to not lose. Performance (one dimensional): Linear. More is better, less is worse, and customers can articulate the trade. Page speed, storage, price. This is where you compete head to head and where most roadmap debate lives. Delight (attractive): Unexpected. Absence costs nothing because nobody asked; presence creates disproportionate love. These are the features that get screenshotted. You will not get them from a survey asking "what do you want," because customers do not know to want them yet. Two more round it out. Indifferent features nobody cares about (kill these), and Reverse features that some users actively dislike, which is exactly why one size fits all settings are dangerous. Category Present Absent Strategy Basic Neutral Angry Meet the bar, no more Performance Happy Unhappy Compete; pick your level Delight Delighted Neutral Where differentiation lives Indifferent Neutral Neutral Don't build it

sections.h: The insight people miss: features decay
sections.t: [!warning] Today's delight is tomorrow's basic Categories are not fixed . A front facing camera was a delighter; now it is a basic, and its absence would be unthinkable. This is the most actionable thing in Kano: a differentiator has a half life. If your strategy depends on a delighter, assume competitors copy it and the market re baselines it into a basic. The The DHM Model question is exactly how long you get to enjoy the delight before it commoditizes.

sections.h: When it's useful, when it's theater
sections.t: I use Kano as a conversation lens , not a scoring engine. The formal version is a survey with paired functional and dysfunctional questions, then a categorization table. It is rigorous and occasionally worth it for a major investment decision. But most teams do not need the survey. They need the vocabulary : "that is a basic, stop gold plating it," and "that is our one delighter, protect the resourcing." The theater version is running the full survey to justify a decision you had already made, or treating the categories as permanent. Pair Kano with The Impact Likelihood Matrix for the how risky axis it does not cover, and with RICE and Scoring Models when you need to rank within a category. One trap to watch for: chasing delighters while basics are broken. Delight on top of a broken basic is lipstick. Fix the login first.

This is the simplest tool in the kit and the one I reach for most on a Monday morning when my list is screaming at me. It comes from a line attributed to Dwight Eisenhower, that what is important is seldom urgent and what is urgent is seldom important, and it was popularized by Stephen Covey. It is a 2×2 that separates two things we constantly conflate: urgency (this needs attention soon) and importance (this moves something that matters). The distinction is load bearing because urgency is loud and importance is quiet. Urgent things announce themselves: the ping, the escalation, the "quick question." Important things sit silently until they are overdue. Left to instinct, you will spend your whole week serving other people's urgency and never touch your own important work. The matrix forces the separation onto paper so you can see the theft happening.

Urgent Not Urgent Important Do (now, yourself) Decide (schedule it) Not Important Delegate (someone else) Delete (drop it) [!note] The verbs, not the boxes Do (Q1): crises, hard deadlines. Necessary, but living here means you are firefighting. Decide / Schedule (Q2): strategy, discovery, relationships, growth. This is where a good PM should live. It is the quadrant that prevents Q1 fires. Delegate (Q3): urgent but not important to you , the work that feels productive but is not yours. This is the hardest quadrant to be honest about. Delete (Q4): the busywork and the meetings that should have been emails. Cut without guilt.

[!tip] Q2 is the whole game Every Q1 fire was, at some point, a Q2 task you did not get to. Discovery you skipped becomes a launch crisis. The roadmap you did not socialize becomes an executive escalation. PMs who feel perpetually reactive are usually living in Q1 and Q3; the ones who feel in control have defended their Q2 time. So treat the matrix less as a sorting tool and more as an argument: spend deliberate hours on important not urgent work before it metastasizes into urgent.

sections.h: Where it fits and where it doesn't
sections.t: Eisenhower is for personal and team triage : your task list, your calendar, the inbox of asks. It is not a roadmap tool, and using it as one is the most common misuse I see. It has no concept of business impact magnitude, effort, or strategic fit, and "important" here is a personal judgment, not a portfolio criterion. For sequencing the actual product roadmap, use The Impact Likelihood Matrix or RICE and Scoring Models. For deciding how much effort a given task deserves once it is on your plate, pair it with The LNO Framework: Eisenhower tells you whether to touch a task at all, and LNO tells you how hard to grind on it. The other failure mode is relabeling everything urgent and important because saying "delete" or "delegate" feels uncomfortable. If all four quadrants are not populated, you are not being honest. The empty Q4 is the tell.

This is my go to for fast portfolio triage, and it is the one I sketch on a whiteboard more than any other. It is a 2×2: impact for the company on one axis, likelihood of success on the other, both high or low. Four quadrants, four very different decisions. I built it because most teams either rank everything in a flat list (no shape) or argue endlessly about a single "priority" score that smuggles two distinct questions into one number. Splitting impact from likelihood forces both questions into the open. The honesty this buys you is the point. A feature can be enormously valuable and a long shot. Another can be a near certain win that barely moves the needle. A flat priority list flattens those into the same bucket. The matrix keeps them apart so you can deliberately balance your portfolio across them.

High likelihood Low likelihood High impact Home Runs Strategic Initiatives Low impact Housekeeping Stuff You Shouldn't Be Doing [!note] What each quadrant means, and what to do Home Runs (high impact / high likelihood): Do these now. Big payoff, and you are confident you can land them. If your roadmap has obvious Home Runs you are not building, stop reading and go fix that. Strategic Initiatives (high impact / low likelihood): The big bets. High value but uncertain: new markets, hard tech, behavior change. You need some of these or you are just maintaining. Fund them deliberately and de risk them with Build Measure Learn and the MVP before betting the quarter. Housekeeping (low impact / high likelihood): Easy, safe, low value. Bug fixes, small debts, table stakes upkeep. Necessary in moderation, but a roadmap that is all Housekeeping is a Product Teams vs Feature Teams slowly dying. Stuff You Shouldn't Be Doing (low impact / low likelihood): Unlikely and not worth much. The name is the instruction. The fact that someone asked for it is not a reason.

[!tip] Triage first, then go deeper This matrix is a coarse filter , deliberately. Its job is to separate the big bets from the busywork in five minutes, kill the bottom right, and show whether your portfolio is dangerously lopsided. It is not precise enough to rank items within a quadrant. For that, drop into RICE and Scoring Models or The Initiative Pyramid. I triage with this 2×2, then score the survivors. The likelihood axis is where it earns its keep over a plain impact/effort grid. Effort is cost ; likelihood is risk of failure , which is a different and often more important question. A low effort feature that probably will not work is still a bad bet. This maps directly onto how I classify Risk Identification and Mitigation: impact × likelihood is the same lens, just pointed at opportunities instead of threats. [!warning] The dishonest likelihood trap The failure mode is optimism. Everyone rates their pet project "high likelihood" because they want to build it. Likelihood has to be a cold estimate grounded in evidence: past results, Continuous Discovery signal, technical knowns, not enthusiasm. If every item lands in Home Runs, you are not estimating, you are cheerleading. The empty quadrants are the tell that you are being honest. One more thing: do not confuse this with the The Eisenhower Matrix. That one is urgency × importance for task triage. This is impact × likelihood for portfolio triage. Same shape, different axes, different job.

Most prioritization tells you which tasks to do. Shreyas Doshi's LNO framework answers a different and badly neglected question: how much effort does each task deserve? It is the antidote to the perfectionist instinct that treats every task as worthy of your A game. That instinct sounds admirable, and it is actually how PMs burn out while their highest leverage work goes half done. Doshi's insight is that effort should be allocated by a task's leverage , not by its presence on your list or by how visible it is. He sorts tasks into three types: [!note] The three task types Leverage (L): Output value is many times the effort you put in. The product strategy memo, the hire, the framing of a hard problem, the customer insight that redirects the roadmap. Pour your best energy here, and over invest, because the returns are super linear. Neutral (N): Output value is roughly proportional to effort. Most routine PM work: a standard spec, a typical review. Do it competently and move on, because more polish does not pay. Overhead (O): Output value stays low no matter how much you put in. Expense reports, status formatting, scheduling, admin. The correct strategy is to satisfice : do the minimum acceptable, automate, or delegate. Effort here is nearly wasted. Type Effort → value The move Leverage Super linear Over invest; bring your A game Neutral Linear Do it well, stop there Overhead Flat / low Minimum viable; automate or delegate

sections.h: Why this is the framework I correct people with most
sections.t: [!tip] Perfectionism is misallocated effort, not virtue The most common and most expensive mistake I see is applying uniform high effort across every task: gold plating the Overhead and Neutral work while the one Leverage task gets your tired leftover hours. LNO reframes quality itself. Excellence is not doing everything excellently. It is matching effort to leverage. A B minus on Overhead so you can deliver an A plus on Leverage is not laziness, it is judgment, and internalizing that is one of the bigger force multipliers in the job.

sections.h: How it fits with everything else
sections.t: LNO operates on a different axis than the what frameworks, so it stacks cleanly with them. Once The Impact Likelihood Matrix or RICE and Scoring Models has told you what to do, LNO tells you how hard to grind on each piece. It is the natural partner to The Eisenhower Matrix too: Eisenhower decides whether a task survives at all, and LNO decides the effort it earns if it does. It also rhymes with Doshi's own The Three Levels of Product Work, where Leverage tasks are usually Impact level work and a lot of Overhead is Optics you can satisfice without guilt. [!warning] The classification is the hard part, and it drifts Two cautions. First, classification is judgment, not a formula, and people systematically misclassify the visible as Leverage because it gets noticed. Visibility is not leverage. Second, classifications are not permanent: a task that is Overhead for a Staff PM may be Leverage for an APM still building the skill. Re sort by your current leverage, honestly. The framework is only as good as the cold eye you bring to the sort.

title: The Three Levels of Product Work
